Review: Shimano's XTR Drivetrain Finally Goes Wireless

Jun 4, 2025
by Dario DiGiulio  
Share
Tweet
You must login to Pinkbike.
Don't have an account? Sign up

Join Pinkbike  Login
436 Comments
Shimano XTR vs SRAM Transmission
0 seconds of 0 secondsVolume 0%

Loading ad
Live
00:25
00:00
23:21
 

Watch here for our semi-scientific comparison of the new XTR and SRAM Transmission.


It's been seven years since Shimano's last major drivetrain launch. The world has changed a lot in seven years, but the bike industry seems to move a little slower, so things are less dramatic in our little bubble. In that seven year period, Shimano's primary competitor released a wireless drivetrain, then completely reworked their wireless drivetrain, then released a cable-actuated version of the same thing. Some tertiary players even got in on the wireless trend, yet Shimano stuck to their slow and steady approach.

At long last, XTR Di2 is here, with a few bells and whistles to differentiate it from the pack. As to how it stacks up against the chief competition, read on.

XTR Di2 Details

• 12-speed, 10-51 or 9-45 tooth cassette
• Wirelessly actuated
• Mounts to standard derailleur hanger
• Compatible with other Shimano 12-speed components
• Groupset weight: 1735g
• Price: $2030 USD (cassette, derailleur, cranks, shifter, chain, battery, charger)
• bike.shimano.com

When it was fresh. Photo: Lear Miller

Though many of the XTR 9200 drivetrain's small details have been updated and reinvented, the core function of the groupset remains the same. The shifting quality is nearly identical to the outgoing 9100 group, and Shimano has been sure to keep the new components compatible with some of the core elements of the old, namely the cassette and chain. The lack of shift cable is ultimately the star of the show here, so let's dig in.



Derailleur
Price: $665 USD / $899 CAD / $1040 AUS
417 grams (long cage w/ battery)
413 grams (short cage w/ battery)

As you can see, the XTR derailleur mounts to a standard derailleur hanger - nothing new on that front. The team at Shimano figured that maintaining the derailleur as the strongest element of the system was the right move, as hangers are readily available and inexpensive, should you need to replace one.

One major change to the architecture of the M9250 derailleur is the omission of a clutch, instead opting for a dual-spring stabilizer that maintains an even tension throughout the gear range. The primary logic here was to reduce the form factor of that main pivot point as much as possible, giving the derailleur more of a wedge-shaped leading edge to prevent catastrophic impacts.


There are two variants of the RD-M9250 derailleur to touch on here: SGS (AKA Long Cage), and GS (AKA Short Cage). Each corresponds to a specific 12-speed cassette, and will cater to a different sort of rider. The Long Cage variant is more in line with what we're currently used to, with a range of 10-51 teeth on the cassette. Short Cage ranges from 9-45t, and will appeal to those who want a smaller form factor and lighter weight at the expense of some top-end ease.
Not all rear suspension designs play well with tiny chainrings, so factor that into your decision if considering your gearing and choosing between the two options.


Both M9250 derailleurs use a carbon cage, with enough overlap to protect the pulley wheels from any strikes. The pulleys themselves are solid wheels, without any holes for sticks or debris to get jammed in.

Solid pulleys, carbon cage.
Battery hatch.

The battery is housed within the main parallelogram, and is accessed through a door on the bottom of that structure. Beyond the benefit of keeping the battery out of harm's way, there's also a potential upside in how much larger that parallelogram itself is, as the pivots are spaced further apart, and the links get a bit beefier.

In addition to being a more robust body overall, the derailleur features an automatic impact recovery function, allowing the derailleur to absorb an impact from a rock, tree, or your buddy's boot, then return to gear without skipping a beat. I've only had this happen on trail once or twice, which is a testament to the small form factor more than anything - I'm certainly not doing an amazing job of avoiding obstacles. In both of those instances, the derailleur let out a little yelp, then zooped right back to gear, taking maybe half a crank to return to its happy place.

Primary function button and battery indicator.
Standard adjustments and battery bashguard.

The XTR derailleur retains the standard B-tension and high / low limit adjustment screws that we're used to, so expect to be using those to get the crispest shifting out of your new drivetrain. As is typical of other drivetrains with the same adjustment, checking tension and spacing on these bolts is critical to maintaining performance.

That battery is rated at 305mAh, with a quoted range of 210 miles of riding. I've found the range to be quite impressive, but more on that later.



Shifter
Price: $220 USD / $298 CAD / $378 AUS
98 grams

The XTR Di2 shifter is an impressively complex thing, with multiple adjustments and functions available to the end user. Most critically, it feels and functions like a regular shifter, with lever position and tactile feedback perfectly mimicking the analog alternative. Both the upshift and downshift levers can be set to one or two click shifting, thanks to a cam mechanism driven by each arm. The first of the two clicks is very light action, with a firmer detent to push into the second. Should you prefer to only get one shifter per push, you can limit that physically on the shifter, or digitally via the Shimano app.

Lever adjustment.
Function button.

The rubberized paddles themselves are very adjustable, with 15° of fore-aft sweep, and 15° of rotation in each paddle. By simply loosening a 3mm at the end of the lever, you can get things adjusted to your liking.

On the face of the shifter is a third button, which can be programmed to perform a variety of functions. In its stock state (and most usefully) it functions as a trim adjust feature. By holding the button for 3 seconds, you toggle into trim mode, and can click the shifter in the direction in which you'd like to adjust the derailleur. Hold for another 3 seconds, and you're back to shifting function. In my experience on trail, 3 seconds is an annoyingly long time, especially compared to the instant nature of turning a barrel adjuster, but the one-handed operation is nice.

My one real gripe here is the lack of a trigger shift function, like that found on most of Shimano's mechanical drivetrains. I've always liked the ability to toggle gears with my forefinger on climbs, as I tend to choke up on the bars a bit when pedaling hard.



Crankset
Price: $310 USD / $406 CAD / $530 AUS
599 grams (170mm length w/ chainring)

Shimano has two cranksets available as part of the larger XTR groupset, with XC and Enduro variants aimed at different user and weight targets. I've been testing the latter: FC-M9220. With 160, 165, 170, and 175mm options available, these hollow-forged cranks should appeal to a wider range of riders than before.

Enduro-specific crank spindle.
The little XTR insignia on the tip of the crank is a nice touch.

They use the same chainring mount as before, though the new 0mm offset chainrings are key if you're hoping to hit the 55mm chainline target. Using an old 3mm offset ring will give you a 52mm chainline, where a new ring on an old M9100 or M9120 crank will give you 55mm.

The M9250 crank uses a standard tension bolt and pinch clamp, like just about every other Shimano crank you've seen, save for last generation's XTR model.



Chain & Cassette
Cassette price: $500 USD / $675 CAD / $860 AUS
368 grams (10-51t)
323 grams (9-45t)

Chain price: $85 USD / $101 CAD / $100 AUS
242-267 grams (depending on length)

XTR Di2 uses the exact same chain as the previous XTR 12-speed group, and features a nearly identical cassette. The latter has a few updated details, with a lighter weight, stronger gears, and improved rivet structure, but the previous cassette will provide the same shifting experience.

This little 9t cog and lockring combo is a fantastic bit of manufacturing engineering.
And the corresponding tool is simple and high quality.

Where the 10-51t cassette uses a standard cassette wrench for installation and removal, the 9-45t requires a new little tool to get the job done. This is critical to getting that very small 9-tooth cog to nest in the standard cassette layout, without adding more weight or complication. In my experience, it's been very easy to work on and live with.



Installation & Settings

The installation process for XTR Di2 is identical to the outgoing drivetrain, with a standard hanger, chain sizing procedure, and derailleur adjustments. Like the previous XTR 12-speed, the wireless generation is sensitive to setup, and benefits from a careful process. There are still engraved markings for checking B-tension, and the standard shop methods for fiddling in all the finer details remain true even when there's not a cable in play.

That lack of a cable makes for an unsurprisingly more digital process, and in that realm Shimano has done an fine job of adding the XTR drivetrain to their E-Tube app. Despite the funny name and sometimes clunky interface, that app has yet to fail me when it comes to pairing to the drivetrain and toggling settings. I'm thankful for this, as the non-app pairing method (which involves holding a few buttons on the shifter and derailleur in sequence) has been spotty at best in my experience.

Speaking of settings, you'll be able to change shifter orientation, alter the 3rd button function, and toggle multi-shift in the app. That multi-shift function even has 5 speeds to choose from, dictating how quickly the derailleur zips up or down the cassette when you hold the corresponding shift lever. I've set mine to dual-click only, with no multi-shift enabled, but to each their own.


Photo: Sterling Lorence

On the Trail

If you've spent any time with the cable-actuated 12-speed Shimano XTR group, then you have a pretty clear sense of how the wireless version works. The shift quality is nearly identical, with a light and smooth quality under all but the highest pedaling loads. Speed of shifting seems to be the priority here, especially over multiple sequential shifts, dancing up the cassette as quickly as you request a shift. In those circumstances, I wouldn't say the shifting is seamless, but the cassette and chain are holding onto each other well enough to get to your desired gear as quickly as possible.

In comparison to the current category leader in wireless drivetrains - SRAM's AXS Transmission - the speed differential is massive. Transmission takes its time getting up and down the cassette, but whether that's a problem or not really depends on the end user. Personally, I've never taken issue with the speed of Transmission's shifting, and have come to appreciate just how methodically and smoothly it drops into gear with each click. XTR is much faster, but doesn't feel as solid with each gear change, especially under very high pedaling load, such as when muscling up a technical climb or while riding an eMTB.

Speed aside, the tactile quality of Shimano's new wireless shifter is superb, with light action and intuitive ergonomics that can easily be adjusted to suit your use case. It may look fairly standard, but the attention to detail here is impressive. I've found that as the miles racked up on the drivetrain, I was using the trim function quite a bit to keep the gearing perfect, as well as checking B-tension with some frequency. This isn't dissimilar to cable-actuated XTR, but does seem to be a fussier service item as you can't give the shifter that extra nudge to get into gear if things aren't syncing up perfectly - the downside of the digital.

You can almost hear it. Photo: Sterling Lorence
Quick shifts on quick trails. Photo: Lear Miller

Shimano's decision to move from a clutch mechanism to the new dual spring design is a bit surprising, and while I can appreciate the longevity the springs are likely to bring to the table, the performance on trail is lacking. Chain slap is pronounced on this new derailleur, and while that's a bike-specific issue to some degree, the issue is still worth mentioning in comparison to other options out there. Deep compressions, harsh landings, and rough trails all elicit quite a bit of movement out of the chain and cage, causing a racket and a noticeable increase in chain movement. Luckily, this is greatly reduced if not eliminated when running the Short Cage version of the drivetrain, as the smaller system seems to generate less slack in general.

As someone that has had issues with clutch mechanisms dying prematurely due to foul winter conditions, I can appreciate the simplicity of the spring. But if noise and chain flail are likely to bother you, I'd recommend opting for the 9-45t drivetrain over the 10-51t, or installing something like an O-Chain or STFU chain damper.

Photo: Lear Miller

With a bit less than 500 miles on the 10-51t drivetrain, it still feels too early to talk about extra long-term durability, though I have been impressed so far. The wedge-shaped derailleur stays out of the way, with only a few knocks to report. Those yielded no meaningful damage or change in shift quality, despite some scuffing on the plastic skid plates. The cassette has some shift scarring, but hasn't developed any rough shift spots or skipping gears. Like the outgoing 12-speed Shimano cassettes, the new one is prone to creaking if not kept clean and installed with just the right amount of grease. In most cases, this is a fixable issue, but on occasion they require a bit more attention than I'd like.

I'll touch briefly on battery life, as its been quite impressive so far. Shimano programmed the XTR drivetrain to stay asleep until awoken by a click of the shifter, so the derailleur draws no battery while being transported on a vehicle, moved around the shop, or while sitting alone in a corner for a few weeks. As a result, the drivetrain has a noticeably longer battery life than AXS Transmission, in our experience so far.

Finally, I want to touch on the Short Cage 9-45t drivetrain, or as I've been referring to it, the Small Block. I don't think this smaller gearing will be for everybody everywhere, but if your fitness and/or terrain allows it, I'd strongly consider this option. I've been running it on enduro and trail bikes alike, and have found the gearing acceptable for the long steep climbs we have in the Northwest. The major upside is the smaller form factor, which is far more clear of obstacles than longer-caged alternatives. The additional benefit of tighter shift spacing and reduced chainslap make for a pretty compelling option.


Pros
+ Very fast shifting
+ Customizable and tactile shifter
+ 9-45t option is excellent for the right rider
+ Slimmer front profile avoids impacts & has sacrificial derailleur hanger

Cons
- Shifting under power not as smooth as competition
- Pronounced chain slap
- Still has a derailleur hanger






Pinkbike's Take

Shimano's new XTR Di2 drivetrain does a great job of bringing their 12-speed shifting performance into the wireless age, with a few compromises made to reach their goals. The overall speed and tactile experience will win some over, but the lack of composure in rough terrain is a sore point. For those looking to shift under maximum load, Transmission or Shimano's own Linkglide will be better options, but the quick shifting and variety of build kit options with XTR Di2 is worth considering. — Dario DiGiulio


Posted In:

Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

two-plank (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:16)
 Someone help me understand the value in wireless for these prices. Genuinely. I just dont get it
  • 3528
HB208 (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:55)
 By no means does someone need it to have fun, but I have Ultegra Di2 on my road bike and the ability to shift up and down the cassette just by holding a button is amazing. About to go up a steep climb and need to shift to a way easier gear? Cool, just hold the shifter down (no need to keep pedaling). Descending and need a way harder gear? Easy.

The value on a MTB is that you can get into the right gear very easily instead of grinding through gears. Whether or not that is worth it to you is a different question.
  • 231
xciscool FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:04)
 @HB208: I think electronic shifting is way more beneficial on road/gravel bikes with a front derailleur.
1x mechanical works great, but with brands like YT speccing electronic shifting on reasonably priced builds I could see ending up with it at some point.
  • 116
HB208 (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:06)
 @xciscool: I personally like it even when I sit in the big chainring on a flat ride. Don't get me wrong, its not necessary at all, but if you have the money I think its worth it if you ride a lot.
  • 300
lowkeyokeydokey (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:07)
 the level of precision and adjustment required is pretty slick, but that's definitely not where I'd invest on my bike. If I had the option I would rather use my money for better suspension, brakes or even tires on a bike over wireless shifting. Comes down to what you prioritize.
  • 101
chochocho (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:11)
 Had a similar mentality as you back then. Not so much the wireless aspect, but Sram's Transmission.
I loved my Sram XO1 mechanical setup and it was always reliable. I initially tried a buddy's bike with first generation AXS Eagle drivetrain at Whistler which was nice, but wasn't sold on fully switching over to wireless.
What really sold me on wireless was Transmission's shifting under load feature. But now that Sram came out with those new mechanical Transmission setups, I probably would've gotten that at the time.

The other benefit for wireless is not having to route cables thru the frame AND if you do a lot of traveling with your bike at the airport, easily removing the derailleur (prob not for Transmission..) to store away when packing is nice too.
You're really just paying extra for convenience.

trailsquatch (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:15)
 I agree, but I don't think the "value" term applies to XTR since it's Shimano's premium offering. As with SRAM, it may be planned to trickle down to more value oriented options in the future.
  • 120
HB208 (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:16)
 @trailsquatch: Yes this means XT Di2 is going to be released soon.
  • 401
KJP1230 (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:32)
 I was an early adopter of electronic 1x12 shifting, and in my view it is not at all valuable. It adds cost, weight and complexity for no discernable performance advantage. Once my original sram electronic derailleur finally died, I replaced it with a shimano XT derailleur and haven't missed it at all. Perhaps there is more value when you are talking about smoothing shifting under e-bike power loads - but for analog bikes I'll take the reduced weight and cost savings all day.
  • 71
HB208 (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:39)
 @KJP1230: Yeah here is the thing, the Shimano road bike systems don't really add weight. This new XTR system is lighter than the previous XTR by 150 grams (which does not justify the cost but it is lighter).

I'm not saying its necessary but I have personally found it valuable on my road bike and would pay more for electronic shifting on a XC bike (not so much a trail or enduro bike).

singletrackslayer (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:46)

  • 41
everythingsucks (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:51)
 @xciscool: that’s how I ended up with it. Reasonably priced build on the bike I got and it came with electronic shifting. I wasn’t looking to change from mechanical at all but I don’t hate electric shifting at all either.
  • 234
skarnold (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:58)
 I’ve been on axs for a bit now and just recently built up a new bike with transmission and to me the biggest advantage of electronic shifting is the lack of needing to fiddle with things. Shift cables stretch over time and shift housing can become damaged or pull through ferrules. Depending on your mechanical ability and the issue this an easy fix (cable stretches play with your barrel adjuster.) but with electronic shifting it just works without having to worry about that stuff which is awesome. Whether or not that’s worth the price point is entirely up to the buyer but for me it’s the main reason I’ll keep going with electronic drivetrains.
  • 1618
thenotoriousmic (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:00)
 They’re for e bikes mainly. The sram t type shifts to slow for actual mountain biking they’re for e bikes where there’s a motor to take the slack when you’re shifting gears and they shift slow so the motor doesn’t chew up your drivetrain. This sounds like it’s more geared towards actual mountain bikes but you can’t fault mechanical for speed. Can’t really see a reason to ever need electric gears on a regular mountain bike.

KJP1230 (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:03)
 @HB208: Fair enough. I supposed I was looking at the offerings from legacy Sram systems, where (for mountain) the electronic versions universally added weight to the cable-actuated versions.

If the new Shimano system is lighter than their cable versions (as well as forthcoming updates to cable versions) - more power to them.

I was speaking purely from the perspective of 1x12 on an enduro style mountain bike. I was excited to try e-shifting, and it ended up being a part that delivered zero added value at additional cost and weight.
  • 271
no-good-ideas (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:12)
 My XT mechanical has never looked so good when comparing initial buy in, as well as wear replacement, cost vs function.
  • 211
jonemyers FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:41)
 I don't know about value but at least for my wife who has thumb arthritis, electronic shifting has been a game changer for her ability to continue riding the mountain bike. The higher force and movement for cable shifting was leading to a lot of thumb pain.
For me, the value is more about the consistant shifting even after a winter of riding on the wet side of the PNW. Mechanical cables eventually get gummed up with grit and dust which leads to poor shifting. Electronic shifting means more riding and less wrenching for me. Also, I must admit that after tearing a thumb ligament years ago, electronic shifting is a lot easier than mechanical for long bumpy rides.
For the most part it is a luxury, but I'd say that for 99% of the world, riding bicycles on trails for fun is a luxury also.

HB208 (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:44)
 @jonemyers: My favorite part of all of these conversations is if a bike should cost $4k or if it should cost $8k. To 90% of people, those are both unattainable.
  • 80
succulentsausage FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:50)
 Not at these prices, but I'm predicting that a wireless Deore derailleur + shifter that's surely coming out soon will be $350 or less, which would actually pique my interest.
  • 160
rltw2-14 FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 12:19)
 Neither do I. I have a good number of friends who are running Transmission and they love how precise it is, but they've also replaced 9 cages between 5 riders and at that point the cost adds up to an insane number (X0 and XX systems).

Then there's me, the holdout. I ran a $200 X01 derailleur for 2 years and just over 4,000 miles before I finally bent the derailleur body and had to replace it. Sure, I've spent time replacing my housing, replacing my cable and doing the periodic b-limit check with the white SRAM tool, but outside of that it just runs - and for 3x less than what others have spent. If you add in additional money for replacement broken cages, I've probably spent 5x less than some of my friends and on the trail there is no noticeable difference between how my ride goes and how there ride goes.

I've ridden Transmission and it shifts wonderfully, but the divide between value and performance is too great and one that makes zero sense to me. This applies go both systems now - too much money for too little gain IMO.
  • 62
pootassium (Jun 4, 2025 at 12:33)
 Buyer's remorse. Granted I've only had it on my road bike, and never found a major benefit. It sounds cool, requires less maintenance/tweaking (?), and pro road riders use it. I actually went back to an old DA. I just want to grab my bike and ride dammit.
  • 103
HB208 (Jun 4, 2025 at 12:43)
 @pootassium: How does electronic shifting not allow you to grab your bike and ride it? Shimano barely requires charging.
  • 208
phops (Jun 4, 2025 at 12:51)
 Wireless is great for 2 things: Drop bar bikes (with ability to shift from multiple hand positions and aerobars), and barspins.

For everything else, its a gimmick. Shifting precision depends on a lot more than the derailleur (chain wear, cassette wear+dirt, muck buildup in the jockey wheels) and indexing of good mechanical systems was never a thing that people complained about.

Furthermore the system has a downsides that nobody talks about - lack of cable housing pressure means way more derailleur movement which equals chain slap which translates into the pedals. This is part of the reason SRAM went to direct mount.

The systems can be good and arguably worth the money, but they need to be able to be programmed for any indexing for any number of gears (which is basically free for the manufacturer as that capability exists, it just needs to be made available in the app). So for example, if you slightly bend a cage or a hanger, you actually can just reprogram the gears and the system should shift fine. There are other features that are easy to implement too, like momentary overshoot for quicker shifting, auto gear shift on low battery, slot for a hex that lets you manually shift to a lower gear in case you don't have battery, and optional usbc port so you can run the system on ebikes off the main battery.

I had an Archer Components system that was able to do all of that, and that system was made by one guy.
  • 102
fireroadflyer (Jun 4, 2025 at 14:00)
 @KJP1230: I am in the same boat. I used a wireless drivetrain and seatpost last season. This year I’m back on mechanical for both. After the initial setup I cannot justify electronic. It looks clean, but for me, the performance advantages don’t justify the expense. I also had 2 rides ruined from dead batteries (1 post, 1 derailleur), which felt really dumb. I’m firmly on team cable moving forward. Mechanical Shimano 11 or 12 speed in whatever price point preferred is very tough to beat. I guess I’m old school, I also prefer a hanger over SRAM direct mount. I keep a spare hanger in my pack. It’s light, and an easy trail side swap if I mangle one in the back country.
  • 121
jerrytek (Jun 4, 2025 at 14:11)
 I've had a SRAM XO Transmission drivetrain on my bike for two years, and have a few months on a second bike with the same drivetrain. Both came on bikes I purchased, so the retail hit for the drivetrain wasn't relevant.

After two years, I see four main benefits:
-It's quiet, with a noticeable reduction in chain slap
-It's very low maintenance. The system just works, and I've never had to adjust anything after initial setup. Initial setup isn't hard as long as you follow directions. Transmission requires far fewer tweaks and micro-adjustments to work properly compared to cable systems.
-It shifts under load better than any other drivetrain I've used. The difference is really stark. It makes you realize how much we have to limit shifts on most drivetrains, with going back difficult.
-It's durable. I've hit my derailleur hard a few times, with absolutely no issues. I haven't had to replace anything. I have a two-year old bike and a new bike with the exact same drivetrain, and the old one works as well as the newer one. If you do break something, the drivetrain in repairable with replacement parts available.

Wireless transmissions aren't mandatory to have fun. The difference isn't as stark as good geometry or upgrades to brakes, suspension, or tires. But it works really damn well. When GX came out the cost of entry went way down. This will undoubtedly happen with XT/etc eventually too. It won't transform your riding (what does, really?). But it does work really well.
  • 20
pootassium (Jun 4, 2025 at 14:56)
 @HB208: Yeah sorry that’s true. On the rare occasion I have run out of juice on a ride but that’s my laziness of not checking the battery.
  • 41
HB208 (Jun 4, 2025 at 15:24)
 @pootassium: I already have to charge a bike computer and light, one more thing is not that hard.
  • 10
pootassium (Jun 4, 2025 at 15:49)
 @HB208: Yeah that’s the drawback to my Di2. Bikes are in the garage with no outlet, easy to grab lights and computer to charge, but not the Di2. SRAM is good for that tho and looks like this XTR too (?)
  • 24
corerider (Jun 4, 2025 at 18:11)
 @HB208: How is this any different from pod shifters with a simple actuation? Moreover, at least with a cable you can hotwire (pun intended) a fix to get home if something goes wrong. Battery dies/electronics don't work and you are stuck in a tall gear.....
  • 10
redfoxrun (Jun 4, 2025 at 19:59)
 @no-good-ideas: amen
  • 11
Mfro (Jun 4, 2025 at 21:05)
 I think this sage gentleman sums it nicely


www.youtube.com/watch?v=8QxIIz1yEsA
  • 21
Super7 (Jun 4, 2025 at 21:08)
 Let's be honest...if you're not racing you don't need it.
  • 50
frorider2 (Jun 4, 2025 at 21:43)
 @HB208: All reviews I’ve read say the new XTR adds 160 g. However I’m not sure they included cables.
  • 40
rob-j20 (Jun 4, 2025 at 22:45)
 @corerider: they don't just die, you get plenty of warning. You start shifting less then when you get your final warning you stay in middle gears if your miles away from home. If the shifter battery dies you can shift the derailleur manually. Plus you can carry a spare battery.
  • 42
oiloikboiks (Jun 4, 2025 at 23:08)
 The shifting on my eeb improved dramatically when I was able to remove the gear housing that had to bend through two 90⁰ corners around a battery and motor.
My road bike has had electronic shifting for nearly 10yrs now, and has never missed a shift or dropped a chain, and works when I've neglected it for months, and is effortless to shift even after I've been riding for 8 or 9 hours straight. All that is valuable to me.
  • 50
mountainsofsussex (Jun 5, 2025 at 0:19)
 @thenotoriousmic: hard agree that transmission is geared towards eebs - on a cable shift you can feel the gear going in or not with your thumb and fractionally back off and you don't really lose time. An eeb motor simply doesn't react with that subtlety, so risks trashi7ng "traditional" drivetrains. XTR wireless seems like a fast shifting gen1 AXS, so let's see how it stands up to the new max power eebs
  • 120
Muscovir (Jun 5, 2025 at 3:00)
 After having owned a T-Type GX Transmission for half a year, I can confidently say I'm not a fan. In my opinion, wireless shifting in general adds a bunch of drawbacks while providing almost no tangible real-world benefits.

Also, I haven't seen this mentioned a lot online, but: I found the reliability of T-Type to be very poor. My GX Transmission needed to be replaced twice under warranty in the span of six months. Both times it just randomly wouldn't turn on anymore. To be fair, SRAM approved the warranty both times without asking any questions and sent me a new derailleur within a matter of days. But having a derailleur that just randomly bricks itself for no reason isn't fun.
  • 42
wyorider (Jun 5, 2025 at 7:07)
 @Muscovir: SRAM doing their thing-pushing a product that should still be in beta testing to market so the fanbois can rave about the innovation. Sucks to test prototypes and pay for the privilege.
  • 12
thenotoriousmic (Jun 5, 2025 at 7:51)
 @mountainsofsussex: @mountainsofsussex: it just doesn’t shift fast enough and constantly leaves you in the wrong gear grinding the crank’s waiting for it to shift. Obviously not a problem when you’ve got a motor to take up the slack. The only reason it shifts so slowly is so ebike motors don’t chew it up. They just don’t want to be honest and say that because they don’t want to alienate the regular bike market.
  • 10
muddered-out-everything (Jun 5, 2025 at 10:03)
 @pootassium: just run a spur to your garage so you can leave all your chargers in there and not have to remove anything from the bike? That's what I do with my commuter for the lights in winter, and running the cable is simple.
  • 30
zmums (Jun 5, 2025 at 11:43)
 @rltw2-14: this is my take too. Works great, better in many ways, just not worth 5x the price.
  • 10
Bosko (Jun 5, 2025 at 18:30)
 Haha. Right? I saw an article that said it was an affordable upgrade. 🤷🏼‍♂️
  • 80
silvbullit (Jun 6, 2025 at 14:06)
 Mountain bikes plateaued in about 2020. Now it's onto pushing marketing to solve problems we really don't have to get you to open your wallet. These wireless drivetrains are creating problems for the future, if anything. 11 and 12 Speed Shimano and SRAM have been dialed for a while now. Cables do it simpler, lighter, cheaper, and without having to worry about battery degradation or corrosion that WILL be an issue 2+ years out. Stockpile high-end analogue stuff before they run out and laugh at the people making payments on their overpriced, under-performing boat anchor drivetrain. Laugh loudly so they feel shame.
  • 20
oiloikboiks (Jun 7, 2025 at 1:52)
 @silvbullit: Boat anchor? You're talking a hundred grams or so difference, at most. Most electronic road groupset are actually lighter than mechanical.
You still get corrosion with mechanical cables. You have indexing issues after a while with mechanical. You still have to replace outer housings, which is a pain on internal routed frames.
I stopped using mechanical groupsets 10yrs ago because I liked how less mentally and physically fatigued electronic groupsets made me feel after big days out (12+ hrs riding) and in that decade I've only had one battery failure and one battery pin failure (which I fixed for 0.02pence and 15mins of time)
Previously to that, I honestly think I've spent more on replacing cables and housings and considerably more of my time maintaing mechanical groups, than any of my electronic groups cost me (I paid £168 for a GX AXS upgrade this week, sold the old GX for £100, so really not much invested)
  • 22027
2004hyuandielantra (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:14)
 Having a hanger is not a con. Paid for by big sram
  • 896
TheR FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:43)
 Agreed. Are we forgetting the reason they came up with hangers in the first place?
  • 5122
mikekazimer FL Editor (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:11)
 That's why it's also in the pros section. There are benefits to having a hanger, since they're cheap and easy to replace, but you also sacrifice some system stiffness. I'd still like to see SRAM or Shimano come out with a derailleur that's even slimmer and shorter - the 9-45 cassette is cool, but it's probably not ideal for riders with really steep terrain, or who to prefer to spin an easier gear.
  • 3439
KJP1230 (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:29) (Below Threshold)
 I disagree completely. The derailleur hanger is the #1 part I break on my bike. The area I ride is reasonably rocky, and its difficult to ride fast and at least have glancing blows to the cage/derailleur which slightly bend the hanger.

I actually think that the more robust attachment style of Sram's Tranmission offering is a bigger innovation than the electronic shifting version. I actively don't want electronic shifting because it a.) offers me zero performance advantage, b.) is more expensive, c.) adds weight to my bike - but I do want a more durable derailleur attachment which can shrug off moderate hits.
  • 321
schu2470 (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:37)
 @mikekazimer: You can't both want a shorter cage and simultaneously complain that the 9-45T cassette isn't enough range. Unless you're going back to a 2x or some internally geared thing to pair with a smaller range cassette to make up for the difference then you get a long derailleur cage to take up the chain slack. It's just physics.
  • 84
PabloMoll (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:04)
 I’ve had so many problems lately with hangers that I’m starting to think to change a couple of frames just because I might upgrade to UDH.
  • 382
no-good-ideas (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:25)
 @KJP1230: so the fact that your hanger brakes and not your derailleur is a negative outcome?
  • 79
Ktr0n (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:25)
 It is but. No more hanger means no more limit and b tension screws. I hate faffing with those, and as the article points out, because it is a wireless drivetrain you also lose the barrel adjuster for small adjustments to keep the shifting crisp as it wears. None of that applies with Transmission.
  • 82
Gibbersticks (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:37)
 @PabloMoll: I would rather pay for a hanger than have to source and pay for Transmission parts. That said the new XTR is fugly and transmission is huge.
  • 82
KJP1230 (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:43)
 @no-good-ideas: Ideally, I would have a derailleur that could shrug off mild to moderate impacts and continue functioning because the design is robust. Bonus points if, in the event of a 6" flood, I can tip my bike over and stand on the derailleur to keep my shoes dry.

Bending a hanger is better than braking a derailleur or frame, definitely. But having a derailleur which can sustain significantly more abuse without bending or degrading in performance, yet will fail in a rebuildable manner before breaking my frame seems ideal.
  • 63
moridinbg (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:44)
 It’s a huge con when it breaks and no shop has one in stock for your brand and then model of a bike in a 500km radius 🤷‍♂️
  • 40
jonemyers FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:47)
 @PabloMoll: I agree with this. I've gotten in the habit of carrying spare hangers when I take longer road trips away from home because it seems like the hanger gets easily bent in transport or when a bike falls over. I've even started to consider bringing an alignment tool. I don't have any bikes with SRAM Transmission, but I'm seriously considering it because of the hanger issues I've seen. It seems like 12 speed drive trains need to be almost perfectly aligned these days to shift well.
  • 20
succulentsausage FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:52)
 @TheR: Modern hangers are way too beefy to bend much less break. I love all my Shimano stuff, but of the 4 derailleurs I've broken in the past 5 years the hanger has been totally intact with no bending/adjustment needed upon installing a new derailleur.
  • 150
Sethimus (Jun 4, 2025 at 12:01)
 @mikekazimer: that's 500% range? 10-51 is 510%, hardly a difference...
  • 22
Tjameson (Jun 4, 2025 at 12:12)
 @KJP1230: FR dude bc rn when I take light or moderate hit on my derailleur (about every ride), BOTH the hanger and the the derailleur bend. The damage to the derailleur is minimal but cumulative impacts mean it doesn't even matter if the hanger is straight. derailleurs purpose built for trail riding (not trying to hit weight requirements for the XC crowd) is definitely the way to go. Losing the hanger sounds great bc its a PITA anyways.
  • 21
vinay FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 12:33)
 @Sethimus: Yeah, still seems huge enough to me. I wonder whether it will work with the microspline cassette. E-thirteen already had a 9t sprocket for a long while but I thought it worked with the XD driver.

Downside of the compact setup might be that loads on the drivetrain will be higher, hence so will probably be drivetrain wear. It may be well worth it for competition though, which is what XTR is all about. I doubt any of these top XC racers really need 510% range and 500% is not enough.
  • 40
KJP1230 (Jun 4, 2025 at 12:40)
 @Tjameson: I'm with you. I literally just replaced my hanger, and after just 6 rides this season its already bent such that I am getting mediocre shifting on 3 cogs.

The hanger is essentially a small lever and is designed to be bendable to protect the frame and derailleur. The fact that it is a lever means that it multiplies the force from a bump or hit as a function of its length.

Hate sram all you want, but transmission-style derailleur attachment is clearly a superior solution to a notable and common problem. The fact that it is rebuildable is also the ethical approach. Good on them for playing the long game to trojan horse the industry into adopting - even better that the derailleurs now come in a cable actuated version at reasonable weight and price point.
  • 53
dick-pound (Jun 4, 2025 at 13:49)
 @mikekazimer: How is 9-45 not enough range? When accounting for the appropriate chainring size for each cassette, the difference is non existing.
  • 90
mikekazimer FL Editor (Jun 4, 2025 at 13:57)
 @dick-pound, it depends on how steep your hills are, and the fitness level of the rider. I like the 9-45 on XC and lighter trail bikes, but I'd rather have an easier gear if I was pedaling a heavy enduro sled.
  • 20
2004hyuandielantra (Jun 4, 2025 at 14:00)
 @mikekazimer: is that not the same gear range as 10-51?
  • 93
Dario-DiGiulio FL Editor (Jun 4, 2025 at 14:03)
 It's 500 vs 510%, but chainrings have something of a lower limit on most full suspension bikes - hence the difference in effective easiest gear.
  • 30
nickfranko (Jun 4, 2025 at 14:12)
 @KJP1230: How the hell do you ride? With your bike 20 degrees from the ground at all times?
  • 81
merlin33 FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 14:25)
 @dick-pound: i run 30 x 52 as my low gear. there are times when i wish i had 28 x 52. i live in pnw, where there are steep climbs.
  • 10
2004hyuandielantra (Jun 4, 2025 at 14:32)
 @Dario-DiGiulio: gotcha, thanks Dario
  • 61
k-n-i-x-o-n FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 14:44)
 @merlin33: I'm not sure I could even stay upright riding as slow as a 28x52 would be
  • 112
mikekazimer FL Editor (Jun 4, 2025 at 15:19)
 @2004hyuandielantra, it's the easiest gear ratio that makes the most difference to me, rather than the range of the cassette. You'd need to run a 28-tooth chainring to get the same climbing gear that you would with a 32 x 51 setup, which won't be possible / feasible for everyone, not to mention the potential effects on the suspension.
  • 61
chrismac70 FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 15:41)
 @schu2470: just use a 28t chainring instead of a 32t and you get the same climbing ratio as the 32/51. At the other end 28/9 is virtually the same as 32/10 and how often do you spin that out
  • 51
chrismac70 FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 15:42)
 @Ktr0n: no more hanger means more sales for sram when you bend their mech instead
  • 45
chrismac70 FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 15:45)
 @Dario-DiGiulio: I’ve never found a frame with a minimum chainring size. Sure they all have a max size for clearance issues. Which frames come with a minimum chainring size requirement?
  • 424
2004hyuandielantra (Jun 4, 2025 at 16:08) (Below Threshold)
 @mikekazimer: hmmmmmm… I don’t really believe in the anti squat/chainring size argument in any major capacity
  • 20
p1nkbike (Jun 4, 2025 at 16:31)
 right? I mean its weird.
  • 30
schu2470 (Jun 4, 2025 at 16:58)
 @chrismac70: I wasn't arguing that 9-45T is a problem. Moreso stating that you can't simultaneously want more range AND a shorter derailleur. We can't realistically go smaller than 9T on a cassette and with the added drag, friction, and wear that's already debatable if it's a good idea or not. I've literally never spun out a 10T cog nor an 11T cog and question whether people who claim to do it on trail actually have. 32x10 @100rpm is almost 29mph with a 29x2.4" tire and that's a relatively comfortable cadence. 32x10 @130rpm is 36mph. Nobody is regularly spinning out 32x10 and certainly not as often as people on here claim to do so.
  • 292
mikekazimer FL Editor (Jun 4, 2025 at 17:01)
 @2004hyuandielantra, I mean, it's literally science, but I'm not going to try to force you to believe in it.
  • 330
2004hyuandielantra (Jun 4, 2025 at 17:39) (Below Threshold)
 @mikekazimer: yeah don’t waste your time, it will fall on deaf ears, go ride your bike.
  • 70
corerider (Jun 4, 2025 at 18:14)
 @mikekazimer: I have used many high end bikes over my riding career and have never found a hanger to have stiffness as an issue. It is an issue because the industry tells you it is. Like wheels. Never talked about wheel sizes being inadequate on the Shore in Freerides heydey until the industry realized by changing wheel sizes they could sell more bikes......
  • 70
corerider (Jun 4, 2025 at 18:14)
 @KJP1230: Sure. Until you break the SRAM derailleur and have to get parts and find out the cost, if they are available. Getting a spare hanger and taking it with you on rides is cheap and easy to do.
  • 81
Super7 (Jun 4, 2025 at 21:52)
 @mikekazimer: Not sure how old everyone is on this thread but 9-45 won't fly for a 55 year old like myself who rides in the Sea to Sky or any other steep area. I'd be down with a custom subscritption which sent me a 9-Age cassette every year on my birthday!
  • 42
minesatusker FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 22:07)
 @PabloMoll: With hangers, i have gone the Aliexpress route and am just stock piling them for all of my bikes. And hanger straightening tool can be about the same price as a new hanger as well!
I will be sticking with Hangers for now and i am still hating the fact that SRAM made bike companies change their rear triangle designs to force customers only to use their groupsets on that bike.
The whole different hangers for different bikes was a pain, (OK SRAM came up with a solution for that), but the whole new hanger/not a hanger system and then selling spare parts for the derailleurs which now could get damaged, as the new hanging system doesn't break. WTF!!???
  • 30
oiloikboiks (Jun 4, 2025 at 23:14)
 @chrismac70: Most suspension linkages are designed with a working range of chainring size. If you go below that range, you put the chain below the main pivot and it affects the suspension
  • 31
oiloikboiks (Jun 4, 2025 at 23:14)
 @2004hyuandielantra: Try it, it's not made up.
  • 20
cbj2012 (Jun 5, 2025 at 4:00)
 @Super7: Yes, and there are a lot of us these days. I am 53 and in good shape but I love my bail out 52 gear.
  • 30
chrismac70 FL (Jun 5, 2025 at 4:21)
 @mikekazimer: does that mean that all the edr riders who swap out the stock 30t chainring and put in a 34t are messing up the suspension for the same reasons?
  • 10
chrismac70 FL (Jun 5, 2025 at 4:23)
 @oiloikboiks: so why is that never published? Presumably putting a bigger chainring on like the edr riders do has an impact on their suspension that none of them worry about.
  • 30
chrismac70 FL (Jun 5, 2025 at 4:31)
 @mikekazimer: I completely agree that if you change the chainring smaller the chainline from the 51t to the chainring gets steeper because the diameter of the smaller chainring is smaller. However if you also change the biggest cog from 51t to 45t then the chain will leave the cassette at a lower height from the axle so the gradient of the chain will lessen back.
  • 10
dancingwithmyself (Jun 5, 2025 at 4:37)
 @KJP1230: Try a NSB hanger. Makes a big difference.
  • 10
oiloikboiks (Jun 5, 2025 at 4:43)
 @chrismac70: It is. It's published in pretty much every manual for a bike. Mine tells me the range of useable sizes the manufacturer recommends.
Because they're racing. They're doing everything to make that bike goes as fast as possible for 5mins, not pedalling it every day for a year. A larger chainring in many cases would benefit gravity riding, but impact pedalling efficiency (which they do considerably less of)
  • 10
cbj2012 (Jun 5, 2025 at 5:09)
 @oiloikboiks: Yes, I remember long time ago that there was talk about chain ring size in relation to pivot placement and then when 1x showed up how going to one chain ring up front was an advantage for frame designers when they did not have to design for 2x.
  • 10
downtownier (Jun 5, 2025 at 5:17)
 @mikekazimer: stiffness where? Why?
  • 10
mountainsofsussex (Jun 5, 2025 at 8:02)
 @chrismac70: but if you do that your chainline is lower relative to the pivot, increasing anti squat (and pedal kick back). Which may be something you like, may be something you don't - all depends on your bike's starting point.
  • 10
vinay FL (Jun 5, 2025 at 12:00)
 @mountainsofsussex: When assuming the same ratio of front vs rear teeth (so for instance comparing the 30-50 to a hypothetical 27-45 teeth situation), the latter may not only lower the top portion of the chain, which does indeed increase anti-squat as you say. It will also tilt this upper portion of chain. Depending on whether the (instant) center of rotation is in front, behind or exactly where the chain meets the upper portion of the chainring, this would amplify, cancel or reduce the aforementioned effect on the anti-squat. It really depends on the suspension design.
  • 10
WheelNut (Jun 5, 2025 at 13:16)
 @mikekazimer: The 9-45 cassette is meant to be used with a 28T chainring so the gear ratios are similar to 10-52 and 32T chainring. Only trouble is that a 28T chainring can muck up your anti squat with many kinematic layouts (high pivot won't have any trouble).
  • 20
WheelNut (Jun 5, 2025 at 14:15)
 @chrismac70: It might not be listed but most frames will have a minimum chainring size. If you go to small the chain will get lower and it'll run into the chainstay.
  • 30
silvbullit (Jun 6, 2025 at 14:14)
 @Ktr0n: you screw up the b-tension every time you remove the rear wheel on a transmission bike unless you futz with the derailleur properly....how hard is it to adjust your b-screw ONE time and it is set forever no matter how many times you need to remove the rear wheel? Elephant in the room is the massive dearth of rudimentary
mechanical ability. Keeping a mech setup shifting well is about the simplest thing on a bicycle. The cables only stretch/housing compress so much and then it's set. Use quality housing and ferrules and it is maintenance free.
  • 11
2004hyuandielantra (Jun 6, 2025 at 16:40)
 @oiloikboiks: no it definitely doesn’t matter
  • 21
2004hyuandielantra (Jun 6, 2025 at 16:41)
 Pedal kickback is fake news if you have any speed at all
  • 10
vinay FL (Jun 7, 2025 at 1:07)
 Then of course we still have plenty of people simply not needing the super light gearing but who may appreciate having access to a heavier gear on a slight downhill slope for that head to head finish line sprint. In the 9sp days, you'd have a triple crankset with a 22t granny and a 44t big ring, paired with an 11-32t cassette. The lightest combo is on par with what's common 12sp gearing now but the heaviest is a massive lot heavier than most are running now. Sure (rear) wheels have grown as well and some cranks have gotten shorter, but I can still definitely see a fit for these 9-45 cassettes even when paired with a 30 or 32t chainring and 29" rear wheels. Heck, if frames have been optimized to run with a certain size chainring, maybe the likes of Nino Schurter have been enjoying less anti-squat than his frame designer initially intended it to have. These smaller cassettes could solve that.
  • 20
oiloikboiks (Jun 7, 2025 at 1:58)
 @2004hyuandielantra: Physics doesn't matter? That's pretty bold.
Ignore gravity, everyone! This guy says it's made up!
  • 10
oiloikboiks (Jun 7, 2025 at 2:01)
 @2004hyuandielantra: Agreed. But we're not talking about kickback, we're talking about antisquat and antirise which IS a real thing that the chain position relative to the main pivot dictates.
  • 20
2004hyuandielantra (Jun 7, 2025 at 11:45)
 @oiloikboiks: pls explain to how a chainring affects the braking characteristics of a bike
  • 10
vinay FL (Jun 7, 2025 at 12:47)
 The chainring has an effect on the antisquat but not on the antirise.
  • 10
oiloikboiks (Jun 7, 2025 at 17:12)
 @vinay: Sorry, newborn at the moment and haven't slept much, might have made an error there, yes.
Not sure why the guy is arguing everything else though
  • 20
2004hyuandielantra (Jun 8, 2025 at 5:00)
 @oiloikboiks: a 2t decrease in chainring size leads to an average increase of 7% of antisquat, this is not perceivable and definitely worth having a tighter gearing, lighter cassette, less spring mass and a smaller derailure thats harder to damage. Yes I am trolling when I say there is not difference, but that “difference” is just not worth the multiple advantages. Hope you can squeeze a ride in and maybe even a goodnight sleep
  • 10
oiloikboiks (Jun 8, 2025 at 14:10)
 @2004hyuandielantra: I don't even care, or remember, what we were arguing about. It's only bikes.
  • 10
vinay FL (Jun 8, 2025 at 21:02)
 @oiloikboiks: Cheers mate, hope all is going well otherwise. It may seem tough now, but it will get better. It is a cycle. Every couple of weeks it will have a super stressed period (and you some nights with little sleep), then a while when it all chills down and you'll be amazed of what new ability your little one has developed. Allow it to change your perspective. When was the last time you went so far outside your comfort zone to learn something new, you were (nearly) crying? If you manage to get a ride in, enjoy!
  • 1121
dirtworship (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:13)
 Yea I'll probably stick with my mechanical XT for like 1/4 the price.
  • 670
dirtmakesmehappy (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:30)

nzandyb FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:26)

slugkiller (Jun 4, 2025 at 12:56)
 Mechanical Deore/SLX with an XT shifter!
  • 20
dirtbaggraeme (Jun 4, 2025 at 15:26)
 @mnguyen1224: been running shimano slx cassette and an XT chain and chainring on a GX derailleur and X01 shifter while I wait for some parts to come in. I'm not convinced I need to wait on the shimano shifter and derailleur. Its not better than full Shimano, but it's better than GX, with that really positive shifter feel but shifts faster, better under load and with a cassette replacement cost that is actually reasonable... it was a bit more of a pain to set up though
  • 60
vitaflo FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 17:35)
 @mnguyen1224: Wide range Deore 10sp cassette, SLX derailleur, Saint shifter is what I run. Crazy good combo.
  • 20
redfoxrun (Jun 4, 2025 at 20:24)
 @vitaflo: 🤯
  • 30
lkubica (Jun 5, 2025 at 2:36)
 Honestly I always used Shimano 11s, switched fo XT 12s on a new bike and it works like sh*t, was shifting ok for like a month, since then changed housing 3 times, hanger is perfectly straight, all mounted according to manual but it simply misses shifts in the middle of the cassette constantly. I was choosing between this and linkglide 11s and I honestly regret going 12s XT, it's the most troublesome drivetrain I had since gong 1x like 12 years ago...
  • 10
dirtmakesmehappy (Jun 5, 2025 at 4:11)
 @lkubica: I had to adjust the low limit screw a few times on mine within the first 6 months of install. Happy days ever since.
  • 20
Schwalbe-Baldy-Bruce (Jun 5, 2025 at 23:55)

  • 20
vitaflo FL (Jun 6, 2025 at 8:18)

dirtbaggraeme (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:41)

  • 125
wyorider (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:18)

freebikeur (Jun 4, 2025 at 13:11)
 Years of waiting and build up just for this... It's as if it took them 6 years to make a first gen Sram AXS
  • 41
tbubier (Jun 4, 2025 at 14:46)
 @freebikeur: agree. Seems odd after all these years they put something out to market that just competes with SRAM rather than surpasses them.
  • 35
R-M-R (Jun 4, 2025 at 16:39)
 @wyorider: Agree. While electronic shifting is undeniably a little nicer than mechanical, the difference is small. The return on investment is a lot higher for fancy suspension or changing tires more frequently.
  • 60
tacopop (Jun 4, 2025 at 18:32)

thevoiceofchaos (Jun 4, 2025 at 19:33)

KolaPanda FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 23:16)

  • 82
Muscovir (Jun 5, 2025 at 3:17)
 I mean, yeah, same as with SRAM Transmission.

And seriously: Why would we be excited about an electronic option in an application where a cheaper, lighter and more reliable mechanical option is perfectly sufficient? Electronic shifting just adds a bunch of weight, cost and complexity for literally no good reason what so ever.
  • 11
wyorider (Jun 5, 2025 at 7:11)
 @tacopop: if the short cage stays tight enough-I’ve had to regrease my clutch on current stuff up to 3x a year. It’s not hard-but it’s annoying when the clutch stops working on a long ride.

Aside from that-a little smoother shifting I guess. I’m on short cage XT now. I don’t want the wheel reinvented, so just some iterative changes please.
  • 702
lepigpen FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:07)
 Yup, that short cage option is a great concept, regardless of wireless tomfoolery. Short cage mechs paired with tiny chainrings is worth its weight in gold for certain riding zones. And riders in those areas have been plagued by the universal 12 speed format for a while now. Wonder if any of them ended up running something weird like a short cage MicroShift.
  • 281
grdn00 (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:19)
 we've been running the 10-45 and short cage 9100 derailleur lol. It's awesome.
  • 73
bluefire (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:25)
 I tried the 10-45 for a while with the last gen and just couldn't settle on a chainring size I liked - it always felt like a compromise. It feels dumb to get excited about gearing, but the 9-45 makes so much sense. Saves a bunch of weight, better chain control, improved clearance at both ends, and no clear downside. Cages have gotten way too long, and the trend towards mullet setups isn't helping.

To me, the smallest sprocket is basically overdrive - I don't need tight spacing at high speed, and I don't think mountain bikers care about chain geometry/efficiency in the top gear like roadies do. Many of Shimano's enduro athletes run the 10-45 right now, presumably for clearance. I wonder if the 9-45 came from their feedback.
  • 120
nfontanella (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:26)
 Shimano has had mechanical short cage 12 speed derailleurs around for a while, it just seems like nobody really talks about them. I'm running it on my xc bike and it's great on more rolling terrain
  • 170
packfill (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:36)
 10-51 and 9-45 are almost the exact same range. It’s 5.1:1 compared to 5.0:1. Someone explain why I’d want to run a longer cage derailleur, if I don’t have to. Just size down on the chainring by 10% and it’s the same thing.
  • 181
AJMIAC (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:36)
 One extra thing to consider: a smaller ring WILL affect your anti-squat, bringing it higher than if you had a normal sized ring. Each bike’s kinematic is based on a certain ring size and deviating from that will make your bike respond differently. In an extreme case, your suspension could lock out or pogo if the AS gets too high
  • 50
shouldknowbettor (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:51)
 I had the short cage XTR for a while with the XT cassette. I like it. The option of a 9T makes it even more appealing
  • 31
jojotherider1977 (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:05)
 @packfill: one thing to consider is that on the faster end of the cassette, you can get spun out with the smaller chainring. I think it can depend on your wheelsize. I had a Capra 29 and ran a 28t and a 9-46 ethirteen cassette. It was just fine and I rarely used the 9t cog. I tried the same setup on a Giant Trance with 27.5 wheels and I quickly ran out of gears.
  • 30
Drew-O (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:14)
 That setup will probably also be popular on gravel bikes paired with road shifters. Honestly kind of surprised that cassette is an XTR product not GRX.
  • 40
PatMAE (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:17)
 I can actually speak to this!

Running Microshift Advent X with a 24T chainring on my trail/xc Banshee Phantom build. Geared for very relaxed climbing paces (I'm a clyde) and still has enough top end for the less hardcore places I take that bike. Yes, sometimes I run out of gearing, but for most of what I ride in non-bikepark season, it's perfect.

Got a Dreadnought running Linkglide XT with a 26T chainring for full-on downhill days. 28T would also definitely work, but there's a lot of fun sidecountry stuff to climb to where I ride, so I've grown to really like the 26T.

I've run a lot of 11 and 12 speed GX setups, but the shift quality degrades way faster than I'd like, and I spend too much replacing exploded derailleurs. Needed something more durable. Both of those setups have been great in my experience.
  • 50
blang11 (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:18)
 @AJMIAC: I learned this only after dropping from a stock 32 tooth chainring to a 30 tooth chainring on my Santa Cruz Megatower. It is noticeable. I mostly notice this as a negative quality during descending with more feedback in my feet. Then of course, O-Chain is not currently compatible with 30 tooth chainrings.
  • 13
preston67 (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:18)

darkstar66 (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:19)
 I think I'll wait until it or slx spec gear hits the shelves before investing in this 😂
  • 10
cycleskiclimb (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:26)
 @packfill: Just to be clear, I agree. It makes for limited upside to the 10-51. The reasons I can think of for longer cage: frame minimum chainring size would be the first that comes to mind. For example, I run a 30t, some frames can't run 28t and even at 28t I'm still a half "gear" away from the lowest (easiest) gear I had in 30x10-51. If low end is important, now you really get into some issues trying to fit 26t chainrings.
  • 30
cxfahrer2 (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:52)
 Still running 11 with e13 9-46 on 30 (Capra 29). The relatively tiny XT 11 derailleur really helps in chunky terrain.

9t cog is a bit harsh to pedal, which can be annoying on smooth surfaces when trying to go fast over a longer period. In a gravel scenario I would avoid it. For enduro it is fine.
  • 30
wyorider (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:16)
 I run short cage XT on both bikes. XC with a 34t ring, trail/enduro with a 30. It works better, hits less stuff, and I really prefer the smaller gearing jumps.
  • 30
davechopoptions FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:22)
 @packfill: bigger chainrings and sprockets are more efficient - matters to some, not to others. Sizing down your chainring may have consequences re anti-squat.
  • 10
g123 (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:23)
 @grdn00: came here to say this, so much yes.
  • 50
Ebergercb (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:59)
 I’ve got 10-45 and really like it. I got it when XTR was the only way to get a GS derailleur, so for the first time in my life I have XTR! Needless to say, I feel pretty fancy.

If I can’t climb something in 30-45, I’ll get off and push. Where I ride, this is plenty.

30-10 is a little low for my biggest gear, but I’m not racing, so I just deal with being spun out sometimes.

If 9-45 takes a new tool, I’ll stick with 10-45.
  • 10
E30 (Jun 4, 2025 at 12:52)
 I run a 28T oval up front and a medium cage XTR with a SRAM 1195 11-42 11 speed cassette. Very light and has decent range. Sometimes I'll throw a Shimano 11-46 on there if I go to the mountains.
  • 40
PremiumCyclingProducts (Jun 4, 2025 at 13:36)
 @Bluefire: The downside is that in the 9t there's a ton of drag. Descending, it's probably fine, throwing a pedal stroke here or there. If you're using it to ride to trails, it feels pretty rough. Even riding in a 10t on flat terrain, you end up feeling the drag on the system.
  • 20
tomo12377 FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 13:47)
 @Bluefire: I have run 10-45 a bit playing around with a 28 & 32t front ring - both were comprises in climbing or outright speed.
What's really interesting here is a 29t front ring on a 9-45 is the same range across the board as a 32t front ring on a 10-51 cassette. Gonna save weight and be more compact across the board - win win
  • 10
bluefire (Jun 4, 2025 at 14:07)
 @PremiumCyclingProducts: I'll have to pay attention next time, but I feel I never use my smallest sprocket on flat ground - only when descending, and generally only descending on a fire road/pavement. That's why I figured the extra drag wouldn't be an issue. 32x10 gets you to 30mph pretty easily, as would 30x9 - might just be that I'm slow, but it's not often that I'm on singletrack at 30mph and feel the need to pedal. For that matter, 30mph on flat ground takes a lot of effort on a road bike, no matter how large or small your sprocket is.
  • 11
boozed (Jun 4, 2025 at 15:14)
 @packfill: If they'd put the 9t on the 51t cassette too it would have been perfect
  • 10
chrismac70 FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 15:48)
 @AJMIAC: so why do manufacturers never publish the minimum chainring size? They always state the maximum but that only for clearance issues rather than kinematic reasons
  • 50
R-M-R (Jun 4, 2025 at 16:47)
 @chrismac70: Because there isn't a minimum for kinematic reasons. The range of pedaling anti-squat values at the ideal sag point is from the low 60% range to over 180%. You could mount a 24T or 38T chainring to most bikes and still be below or above, respectively, the values that other designers have chosen as their ideal.

If we discard the extreme values and limit ourselves to one or two standard deviations from the averages on suspension properties and chainring sizes, the above statements remain true.

That said, you may not like the feel of your bike with a dramatically different chainring size, but there probably exists another frame with otherwise similar fit, handling, and suspension properties that will have your preferred pedaling properties with your preferred chainring size.


@davechopoptions: The efficiency doesn't change much with chainring size, but yes, it changes dramatically with cassette sprocket size on the smaller sprockets due to the relative change. For example, one tooth difference is trivial on most sprockets, but when we're getting down to 9 vs. 10, you really notice it.
  • 20
Muscovir (Jun 5, 2025 at 0:34)
 On my Specialzed Status I've been running the Saint 10-speed setup with an 11-38 SunRace cassette for a while. You probably can't get a better ratio on the spectrum of "short derailleur cage" and "wide gearing range" than this. Lots of ground clearance, perfect for mullet setups. Downside: Saint is quite expensive and I don't think SunRace sells that particular cassette anymore.
  • 21
lkubica (Jun 5, 2025 at 2:41)
 @packfill: No, because your frame antisquat depends on the chainring size, typically calculated for 32t, going for 30t or less will cause more bobbing going uphill, this is the only reason to use 10-51 instead of 9-45. Also the 9 cog wars out 10% faster, but for mtb this does not matter at all.
Also the PK gets exaggerated by smaller chainrings (when measured as a degree of rotation).
  • 10
Muscovir (Jun 5, 2025 at 3:22)
 @lkubica: It works the other way around though. Going for a smaller chainring up front will change the position of the chain relative to the location of the main pivot so that the anti-squat increases. Conversely, going for a bigger chain ring will decrease anti-squat.
  • 10
chrismac70 FL (Jun 5, 2025 at 4:25)
 @R-M-R: But Mike and Dario are arguing that using a smaller chainring does harm the kinematic of the bike
  • 10
chrismac70 FL (Jun 5, 2025 at 4:27)
 @Muscovir: that’s true if you keep the same cassette but if your largest cog is now a 45t and not a 51t then the chain line will change the opposite way because it’s coming off a smaller rear cog
  • 10
GZMS (Jun 5, 2025 at 6:15)
 @wyorider: on the new 9-45 jumps are bigger than on 10-51
  • 10
GZMS (Jun 5, 2025 at 6:17)
 @wyorider: also smaller sprockets have more drag
  • 21
PremiumCyclingProducts (Jun 5, 2025 at 10:12)
 @Bluefire: I guess that just furthers my point that the 9-45 is a stupid product. If you already don’t use the 10 why would you want a 9? The 10-45 has been around for quite a while and is likely a better option for most riders.
  • 10
bluefire (Jun 5, 2025 at 10:48)
 @PremiumCyclingProducts: Well, I never use it on FLAT ground. I use it riding home from the trail on busy downhill streets. In that situation I'm not worried about efficiency, just top speed - I want to at least hit 30 before spinning out. My previous 10-45 and current 11-50 (Linkglide) are both around 450% range, so I geared both to get to 30mph at the expense of my lowest gear. With the 9-45 at 500%, I could have a lower low gear with a smaller ring and still have the 9t overdrive I like for riding with traffic.

I guess that's pretty specific to me and where I ride, but that's my thinking. Describing it now, sacrificing my low gear for a high gear on a mountain bike sounds kind of crazy. I wonder if the new GS cage derailleur will work with the existing 10-45 cassettes for those who prefer them - or if the 9-45 will work with existing GS mechanical derailleurs?

On my gravel bike, I run GRX 1x with the 10-51 cassette. I tried the 10-45 and it definitely wasn't enough for that application - 30mph isn't fast enough for something with drop bars, but I need the low end for bikepacking. I think gravel bikes actually need range more than mountain bikes do - and if the growing prevalence of T-Type mullets on race bikes and OE spec are anything to go by, the industry agrees.
  • 20
R-M-R (Jun 5, 2025 at 11:29)
 @chrismac70: "Harm" is the problematic word. "Change", undoubtedly, but not every bike has every rider's preferred pedaling kinematics with the stock chainring size, so changing the chainring will "improve" the pedaling kinematics for some riders' taste and "harm" it for others.

The problem originates from an assumption that the original configuration is perfect and must never be altered. As a bike kinematics designer myself, I can tell you the original values come from my own preferences, the preferences of the bike's product manager (if I'm not also acting as the product manager), and/or a market survey of competitor's properties. We don't derive a perfect number from immutable laws of physics; it comes from fallible humans with personal preferences.

@lkubica: No, changing the chainring size does not guarantee more pedal bob. Downsizing will increase the pedaling anti-squat, which will usually reduce pedal bobbing, increase kickback, and decrease traction in extremely difficult situations - the typical suite of changes that come with increased pedaling anti-squat, as would've been the case had the designer(s) chosen a higher pedaling anti-squat value for the frame. This will be a positive thing for some bikes and some riders or a negative thing for others - but no matter what, it's not a huge change and is almost certainly well within the normal range of pedaling anti-squat values chosen by other designers.

@PremiumCyclingProducts: I agree most cassette ranges are more about making an impressive spec than a necessary product. Whether that's 9-45 or 10-51+, modern cassettes - with an appropriate chainring - offer me a climbing ratio so low I can barely stay upright at that speed and a top ratio so high I use it only on pavement descents ... and that's more than necessary. Personally, I can live with spinning out on pavement a couple times per season, so your suggestion of 10-45 would also suit me.
  • 10
ebikepartyshirt (Jun 5, 2025 at 22:36)
 @R-M-R: Do you think going 32 to 34 tooth makes a noticeable difference to a bikes antisquat? Dropping the AS to be more sensitive?
  • 20
R-M-R (Jun 5, 2025 at 23:08)
 @BrianColes: On a typical design, that chain in chainring size changes the pedaling anti-squat roughly 5%. It's possible a sensitive rider may notice this, but keep in mind:

• Pedaling A-S varies between popular bikes by close to ten times as much
• For a given model, pedaling A-S varies between sizes by several times as much due to the difference in rider centre of mass location (unless the model in question uses size-specific kinematics, a concept I'm not too modest to say I introduced!)

So yeah, it's possible you could notice it, but it's minor, it's smaller than other factors that people don't get worked up about, and it's equally likely you would prefer the change as dislike it - if you even notice it.
  • 20
R-M-R (Jun 5, 2025 at 23:13)
 @BrianColes: And please pretend I proofread my responses
  • 623
turtletim3 (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:22)

vtracer (Jun 4, 2025 at 18:36)

rob-j20 (Jun 4, 2025 at 22:48)

dougfs (Jun 5, 2025 at 1:31)

Muscovir (Jun 5, 2025 at 3:24)

bluefire (Jun 5, 2025 at 7:45)

Some, like Pinkbike, found the new spring system noisier than a clutch, while others specifically called it out as quieter and better-controlled than the last gen - the exact opposite. Could that be due to time spent on the GS vs SGS system? Or maybe a difference in setup, or variation from bike to bike? Weird one.
  • 365
bigmeatpete420 (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:26)
 I’d love if you could respond Dario, but how is having a derailer hanger a con? That means backwards compatible with just about any bike in history which is a good thing because not everybody gets a new bike every year to keep up with standards. An old Shimano works just fine with the derailer hanger and this one still does putting that as a con is crazy work
  • 140
z-man (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:46)
 I agree, I see it as a huge plus.
  • 21
spacedoutboy (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:18)
 I guess with direct mount it’s a bit easier to set up, since there’s no need to set high, low limit screws and B gap.
