NealWood (Jun 4, 2025 at 13:06)
 Shown as both a plus and a con depending on your perspective.
  • 11
chrismac70 FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 15:54)
 It’s also good for sram as it means you bend their product rather than the mech hanger so more $$$$ to sram
  • 10
jcougs (Jun 5, 2025 at 18:19)
 @chrismac70: who do you think makes the universal derailleur hanger that 95% off bikes run these days? It’s not Shimano…..
  • 4518
solarplex (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:22)
 Sounds like a decent axs competitor but no transmission.

Not sure we expected much more out of shimano unfortunately.

Dont get me wrong. I love my mechanical xt 12. I cant see myself having transmission on a bike but for the arm chair arguments…. This is not on par with srams latest offering.
  • 385
bluefire (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:45)
 With no dog in this fight - I too prefer mechanical on my MTB - I think there's a strong case for Di2 > T-Type. This XTR seems to address a number of the key complaints I've seen with Transmission, as well as that system's been received.

- Damage or loss of AXS battery on XO/XX -> larger, fully enclosed battery
- Unusual/uncomfortable shifter ergonomics -> traditional, highly-adjustable shifter
- Closed ecosystem -> full backwards compatibility
- Restricted, slow shifting speed on T-Type -> no restrictions, fully customizable shift speed. The consensus has been that T-Type achieved shifting parity with Shimano 12sp mechanical, so if this is essentially the same system, any decrease in shift quality would be a result of Shimano leaving control to the rider and allowing you to force it.

As far as UDH vs a traditional hanger - that seems like a philosophical difference to me. SRAM's design seems built to take a punch, whereas the XTR derailleur seems designed to dodge it. I admit I'm a little disappointed to hear that the new spring design doesn't control the chain as well as a clutch with the 10-51t cassette. With that in mind, I think anyone interested in this group should buy the 9-45 GS version. I don't see any real downside.
  • 1812
chrismac70 FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:47)
 Sounds way better than transmission to me. It’s cheaper and the shift showed difference is massive. You will be on the next section of trail before the transmission mech has finished shifting
  • 399
kashimadaddy FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:50)
 Out of curiosity, what makes you say that? I've had transmission on two bikes for a year now, and in every conceivable way it's a downgrade. Slow, clunky shifting, massively exposed derailleur that constantly gets hit and is astronomically expensive to rebuild ($18 . I've also had my GX derailleur just go completely "brain dead" on two occasions. It was warrantied both times, but still annoying to not be able to ride my bike.

Lack of a hangar is also very annoying.

I don't get why anyone thinks Transmission is the standard. Everyone I know who has it doesn't like it and has been plagued with issues similar to my own.
  • 68
Bikesbecauserunningsucks (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:10)
 @kashimadaddy: Transmission is not designed for the gravity crowd. For XC people though, it’s brilliant. In the stand I agree that Transmission is pretty slow and thoroughly unimpressive. There’s something super cool about it getting smoother the harder you pedal though. When I used to race it was incredible. Mechanical shifting can’t hold up to the same load in the slightest. But yeah, for casual riding stick to something cheaper and faster between cogs.
  • 180
solarplex (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:15)
 @kashimadaddy: alright ive read some more reviews and it seems like they have specifically did things not because of lack of innovation but to directly take on issues with transmission.

Standard hanger
Bashable
Standard cassette
Normal chain
A normal shifter
Faster shifts

I see thay they seem to have nailed it based on other bike review pages.

The loam wolf has a video of this shifting and its fast as f compared to transmission so i retract my og reply.
  • 50
Austink (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:46)
 @Bikesbecauserunningsucks: this is what I was going to comment. It is slow because it is waiting for the ramps to line up. So pedaling faster speeds up the shifting. When I am on my xc bike hammering, I don’t even notice the shift happening. On my enduro bike where I typically have a lower cadence, it is more pronounced. When I am racing my enduro bike and pedaling faster, I don’t notice it again.
  • 30
bluefire (Jun 4, 2025 at 15:41)
 @Austink: There's definitely a difference between single-shift and multishift speed though. Transmission (and Linkglide) have fewer shift gates than traditional drivetrains, so they're just a little slower on a single shift, but yeah, when the cassette is turning faster, the chain will move over to the next sprocket faster. I think this is a pretty marginal difference.

I think when people complain about slow shifting on (electronic) Transmission, their problem is that T-Type rate-limits you - you can't grab or dump a bunch of gears quickly. You can press the button as fast as you want and as many times as you want but the derailleur is only going to change gears at the rate that it wants to, which is pretty slow. That's how you end up stuck grinding up a sharp hill in a tall gear, waiting for the derailleur to catch up with you. Mechanical*, though, and now Di2, will let you shift as fast as you want, and if you break the chain or take teeth off the cassette that's on you.

*excluding mechanically limited shifters for e-bike specific drivetrains, Linkglide/Cues, Eagle 70/90
  • 20
bluefire (Jun 4, 2025 at 15:52)
 @Bikesbecauserunningsucks: This is the whole reason I bought X0 when it came out - the promise of an indestructible chain. I've been breaking chains and cassettes (and freehubs, spokes, etc) ever since I started taking cycling seriously, and SRAM seemed pretty confident that T-Type would end that. To me that was easily worth the high cost of entry.

But I broke two chains in the first 200 miles, and the people around me started breaking chains too. I quintuple-checked my setup. I was willing to put up with the rate-limiting rear derailleur, cost, battery and the extra weight if it meant I wouldn't have to deal with broken chains, but without that, they were just a hindrance.

So I swapped to 11sp mechanical XT Linkglide, and I haven't had a single issue in close to 700 miles, with a pretty dubious maintenance schedule. The shifter action feels pretty unrefined, but I love how responsive and natural mechanical shifting is - and as far as the actual drivetrain, Linkglide has delivered on the promise T-Type couldn't keep.

Having said all that - since (analogue) XTR Di2 is based on the HG+ architecture, rather than Linkglide, I won't be buying it. I wish there was a Linkglide option for analogue bikes but I get why there isn't. Sounds like there is a higher-end XTR Linkglide cassette on the way, though - I will *definitely* be getting that.
  • 30
Bikesbecauserunningsucks (Jun 4, 2025 at 16:36)
 @Bluefire: I’m glad you found a setup that works for you.

For whatever it’s worth, the riders I’ve worked with have also blown up some XO chains. No such issues on XX and XXSL. I’m not sure if they’re made differently or what. I know that “buy a more expensive drivetrain than the already exorbitant XO” is not the most savory plan… its just been my experience.

Like I alluded to earlier, people outside the XC racing side of the sport are best served by other groups. Sram’s mechanical is still excellent and while Shimano isn’t my cup of tea, I understand why people like it.
  • 20
Hugou4BdaD (Jun 4, 2025 at 22:00)
 @Bluefire: The XTR M9260-11L is what you're looking for. Although the media outlets published nothing about it, if you check the Shimano website there is a Linkglide version available.

bike.shimano.com/products/components/pdp.P-RD-M9260-11L.html

The complete list of components can be found on this link:
bike.shimano.com/products/components.disciplineLv1_mtb.series_xtr.seriesGeneration_xtr_m9200.html
  • 10
bluefire (Jun 4, 2025 at 22:10)
 @Hugou4BdaD: I wish there was a way to make that work with a pedal bike! But that one has to be connected to an e-bike's powertrain. Still have to wonder if there's some way for me to hack the 12s GS and e-11s SGS versions together to get what I want.
  • 11
minesatusker FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 22:13)
 @solarplex: Basically Shimano has stuck with what it does well and made it compatible for bikes which haven't jumped onto sram train.
  • 20
Hugou4BdaD (Jun 4, 2025 at 22:59)
 @Bluefire: Yeah. I just noticed that they didn't release the Linkglide version with a built-in battery. My bad.

I was just thinking. The only difference between the derailleurs is the software. They could simply allow the user to choose the system that he will ride using the app. Just a dream, because it won't happen.
  • 212
Gwizard FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:13)
 Wow i knew it was going to be expensive, but that is pretty pricey. 500$ for a cassette is a tough sell. Would’ve made more sense to release this in conjunction with a cheaper model, cause sram already has a tiered price point.
  • 211
somebody-else (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:28)
 $660usd for XX Eagle Transmission cassette. F that noise!
  • 142
atourgates (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:41)
 I just had to look again to confirm, but this pricing is insane.

SRAM's transmission XO is $1,599 MSRP ($430 cheaper than XTR). You can buy one right now from Universal Cycles $1,099, and it's been that price since at least October of last year when I bought one.

Even I, a self-professed weight weenie, would have a real hard time paying that kind of a premium for a bit of weight savings.

I'll admit that I don't love how slow T-Type is, so faster seems nicer, but honestly, for the price I'd probably just go back to pre-T-type AXS for faster shifting (I don't really find that "shifting under load" works in any practical sense with T-Type).

I think the bike industry as a whole is better when there's real competition, but this seems like about 5-years late, and about $1K too expensive.
  • 112
bluefire (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:56)
 @atourgates: I think an eventual XT Di2 will be the X0 competitor. XTR has some carbon (blades, cage) so it would fall closer to XX or XX SL than X0, although it doesn't have carbon cranks. If you look at the costs of the other components this looks a lot more competitive. I don't see any weights in this article. I have a feeling tariffs also may have bumped XTR's pricing before this launch.

Derailleurs: $620 XTR vs $715 XX
Shifters: $205 XTR vs $150 XX
Cassette: $465 XTR vs $660 XX
Chain: $69 XTR vs $140 XX

$1359 XTR vs $1665 XX, excluding cranks
  • 40
solarplex (Jun 4, 2025 at 12:09)
 You can use a deore to xtr current cassette
  • 51
Brave1i1toaster (Jun 4, 2025 at 12:22)
 @atourgates: I recently demo'd a bike with a XX SL groupset, after just finishing a ride on my XX1 AXS equipped bike. Holy shit, that shifting speed is atrocious on the SRAM transmission system.
  • 30
chrismac70 FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 15:55)
 @atourgates: Xo is xt level so let’s see how that compares when it comes out
  • 20
Gwizard FL (Jun 5, 2025 at 3:53)
 @somebody-else: haha i probably forgot about that as a trauma response
  • 10
somebody-else (Jun 5, 2025 at 4:11)
 @Gwizard: I can see why lol
  • 279
wolftwenty1 FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:13)
 So shifts faster but not under load...and more expensive...hmmm..
  • 40
TheR FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:48)
 All these systems have said they’re designed shift under load since Sram’s 2x10s in 2011. But do they really? I admit I haven’t tried Transmission yet, so maybe it does. I can’t complain too much about my X01 mechanical, and it shifts under a load to a degree, but you better not put too much of a load on there.
  • 35
chrismac70 FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:49)
 It’s cheaper than sram and the shift shed is so much faster you will be onto the next section of trail
  • 60
JSTootell (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:07)
 @TheR: My 9100 does shift under load very well. Not leaps and bounds better than the X01 mechanical on my other bike, but it is better.

I didn't notice the difference until I tried running a Garbaruk cassette for a while and found the quality to be pretty low. But pretty well manageable if you know how to shift properly. But I can turn my brain off pretty well on the XTR cassette.
  • 31
Austink (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:53)
 @TheR: transmission really does allow you to shift under full power. I don’t even think about when to shift anymore. I try the same thing when riding my non transmission bikes and it is definitely more clunky.
  • 41
litany (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:11)
 @TheR: transmission shifts under load. Even on an e-bike in boost it shifts great.
  • 52
wyorider (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:25)
 I can shift under power on XT 12, and it works great. There was some journalistic bias against Shimano’s lighter feel in this review. SRAM cassettes have fewer shift points and taller teeth, I’ve still seen a lot of GX cassettes where one of the cogs is peeled off the spider under power.

Shimano is as (or more) reliable shift for shift, and there’s less drivetrain shock. If you’re trying to put the watts down smoothly on rolling terrain, it’s much better.

And honestly-modern SRAM is good enough I wouldn’t swap a drivetrain on a new bike if it was GX or better.
  • 40
CobyCobie (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:35)
 @TheR: My XT chain and SLX cassette shifts smoothly under a near flat out sprint.
  • 52
succulentsausage FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:56)
 Breaking news! The highest end Shimano groupset is more expensive than SRAM's 3rd from highest end groupset. Can't wait to see how low they get the price on the new Deore.
  • 20
TheR FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 15:12)
 @Austink: To you and all the other guys testifying that transmission shifts under load — that’s good to know, and maybe that makes the thing worthwhile. I remember the SRAM guys doing a demo day for their 2x10 in like 2011 or so. They were like, “Get out there and really shift on it — give it all you’ve got! I ended up bending a hanger and snapping a cable. Since then, I’ve had an XT 1x11 and two X01 12 speeds. They do shift OK under a load and they’ve gotten better, but still not total. I’ll give transmission a go.
  • 10
chrismac70 FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 15:57)
 @Austink: it allows you to shift when the transmission decides it’s not going to explode.
  • 214
devinkalt (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:21)
 Yeah I think they missed on this one
  • 229
sprung-mass (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:59)
 Getting rid of the clutch is a big fail on Shimano's part especially for enduro riding where you have lots of big hits. Transmission with the direct mount (lack of B pivot) and their new strong and replaceable clutches is a clear winner here. Sad it took them 6+ years to release this mediocre product.
  • 34
wyorider (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:26)
 Short cage, bump your chainring down 2 teeth.
  • 72
succulentsausage FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:58)
 @wyorider: Pay tons of money for a component and then have it introduce compromises on other components??
  • 11
nfontanella (Jun 4, 2025 at 17:12)
 @wyorider: then you lose your top end
  • 11
wyorider (Jun 5, 2025 at 7:16)
 @nfontanella: I’ve never missed the “top end” gear on the big bike. Ever.

If you can turn 130 rpm you still have a 60+ kph top gear.

If the terrain is chunky you aren’t pedaling.

XC bike benefits from harder gearing-but that has more to do with chainline-more watts, smoother terrain, so usually in a harder gear.
  • 10
wyorider (Jun 5, 2025 at 7:18)
 @succulentsausage: short cage solves problems. More derailleur clearance, less weight on the rear axle.

If I did an AXS build I’d probably do an XPLR cassette and rear derailleur too.
  • 10
nfontanella (Jun 5, 2025 at 10:09)
 @wyorider: depends on the trails you ride, I use the 9t and 52t gears within the same ride pretty regularly
  • 192
ethanrevitch FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:48)
 Wireless Shimano before GTA 6 is crazy
  • 130
jankslayer (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:29)
 Would like to see raw aluminum finished crank arms and an updated mechanical derailleur for the new 9-45 cassette. Honestly 1x12 mechanical XTR is still the best groupset going if you consider cost, weight, reliability, livability and performance.
  • 10
wyorider (Jun 5, 2025 at 7:20)
 That would be XT-close on weight, just as smooth, just as durable. And you can just sub in a lighter chainring and cassette to get within a whisker of XTR weight for less $$. And you can sub in Deore brakes and SLX crankarms to save that money back.
  • 10
jankslayer (Jun 5, 2025 at 11:44)
 @wyorider: somewhat, the XTR Rd shifts better and doesn’t have the easily bent cage XT and SLX have. The XT and SLX brakes have bad wandering bite point, never had it on any XTR’s. XT cassette and cranks are good. May as well get the XTR chain and BB as the price difference is minimal. The XTR shifter is also far nicer to use than XT or SLX. Deore everything is really sweet for the price, it’s just real heavy.
  • 131
bproelofs (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:18)
 Seems like a lot of expense and tech to not use a simple cable. Doesn't seem like any appreciative advantage over the excellent Shimano mechanical stuff but costs a ton, rattles and you can destroy a crazy expensive derailleur with no rebuild. I don't get it. The 9-45 cassette is cool though.
  • 130
brajal FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:13)
 Is that hard to make a non friction mark on a crank design? Shimano is not alone tho

nozes (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:22)

WhateverBikes (Jun 4, 2025 at 12:30)

  • 130
nozes (Jun 4, 2025 at 14:52)

  • 30
forgotmyloginagain1 (Jun 4, 2025 at 16:06)

WhateverBikes (Jun 5, 2025 at 0:01)

  • 10
WhateverBikes (Jun 5, 2025 at 0:04)
 @nozes: 970 is nice, so I kinda agree there, but the 950 is pretty bland too.
Anyway, my point was that there's a difference between something being 'meh', with no standout design, or being 'hideous'. Granted, I'm not a fan of big logo's either, but I still would call this crankset extremely ugly just for that.

socalryder (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:55)

JSTootell (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:10)

HB208 (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:14)

  • 91
Mtmw (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:52)
 I don't understand the conversation about weight. From the article:

RD Weight:
417 grams (long cage w/ battery)
413 grams (short cage w/ battery)

The old cable driven XTR RD weight was 242g:
bikerumor.com/hands-on-complete-shimano-xtr-m9100-1-x-12-group-with-actual-weights

So you're gaining half a pound of sprung weight. If you want to take the weight savings from the rest of the group, go get the rest of the group. But that RD is a pig and half a pound is insane porkiness. Wireless shifting again only for porky ebikes.
  • 80
marxrydz (Jun 4, 2025 at 16:08)
 When can we just go back to polished silver cranks if they are metal? the graphics are already rubbed off of the ones in these photos. If nothing else put the black part/graphics in a location where heel rub isn't going to make it disappear in no time. My 2009 DuraAce pure polished silver crankset still looks brand new. Same with anyone that is running the polished Shimano DXR BMX cranks.
  • 112
2socks (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:17)
 A 45t cassette is a good option for 27.5/MX setups.

Good thing that shimano makes a 10-45t mechanical 12 speed, because f*ck electronic derailleurs.
  • 21
R-M-R (Jun 4, 2025 at 16:34)
 45 is great for most people, in my opinion; just downsize the chainring as necessary.

Most people choose their chainring by the largest low ratio they can live with, usually resulting in several of the highest ratios being rarely - if ever - used. Instead, I favour choosing the smallest top ratio they can live with, which rarely results in the lowest ratios being unused (let's be honest, everyone has days when there's no such thing as a too-low ratio for some sections of a trail).

While it's true this increases the pedaling anti-squat, my enduro bike has a 29" rear wheel, 28 × 9-45 drivetrain, and close to the highest anti-squat on the market - and I like it. Pedaling traction in rough and slippery terrain isn't ideal, but elsewhere it's noticeably more efficient than bikes with low pedaling anti-squat and high traction. There are so many options out there that a person can choose a frame with pedaling anti-squat that will combine with a smaller chainring to produce the pedaling properties they prefer.
  • 10
hubertje-ryu (Jun 4, 2025 at 22:26)
 @R-M-R: I can confirm. Still riding a 10 spd 11-36 cassette with a short cage mech and 28 teeth up front, and I’m not giving anything up to the big gearers.
  • 10
R-M-R (Jun 5, 2025 at 11:43)
 @hubertje-ryu: We should all choose the range that suits our bodies and our terrain, and I'm glad you've found a compact ratio that works for you. It may be relevant that you live in Belgium
  • 101
grdn00 (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:18)

JrBones (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:08)

bluefire (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:30)
 I get the joke, but I'm seeing it on sale online now. Full credit to Shimano for that one.
  • 60
Huktophlat (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:29)
 Dario mentions chainring size having an impact on certain suspensions. Went from a 32 tooth to a 30 on my Tallboy so i could have a smaller cassette / derailleur combo and i swear the suspension is noticeably worse. am i crazy or is it a real thing?
  • 40
blang11 (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:23)
 Did the same thing on my Megatower and while I like having an easier climbing gear for brutal steep pitches, the suspension gives more feedback through my feet. It's a tradeoff.
  • 51
pmhobson (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:50)
 It is a real thing. It all comes down to the how the chain tension aligns (or doesn't) with the linkages
  • 30
Austink (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:28)
 As someone who had both a tallboy and a megatower I noticed this as well when going from 32 to 30. My thought is that vpp has noticeable pedal kickback and with a smaller front ring it needs to rotate more, thus making it more noticeable. It is crazy that my 130mm druid is less harsh through rocks than my 160mm megatower was. High pivot has very little pedal kickback.
  • 10
jcav5 (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:53)
 weird. did you switch back and notice an improvement?
  • 10
vitaflo FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 13:46)

wolftwenty1 FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 19:28)

grm (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:07)

St33zusChr1st FL (Jun 5, 2025 at 8:58)
 @cbj2012: So Shimano has still not really said anything about this, but I did just get to talk to my local Shimano rep about it and he said that the new XTR di2 will be backwards compatible with the current GRX 12spd di2 lever. Gravel riders rejoice!

brookscurran FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:12)
 No standard is safe. Not even the age old locking tool that’s even becoming standard on suspension.
  • 126
mrbrighteyes FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:14)
 The biggest negative to AXS was the chain slap due to the lack of cable pushing down on the derailleur... and this is worse?? No thanks.
  • 133
brookscurran FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:19)
 I haven’t had any chain slap
Issues with transmission
  • 122
mrbrighteyes FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:20)
 @brookscurran: Transmission =/= AXS. Transmission eliminated the chain slap issues by going to a hanger-mounted setup.
  • 10
Ty927 (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:03)
 @mrbrighteyes: I had tons of chain slap with XO transmission on my Hightower, like real bad, reminded me of riding with pre clutch derailleurs.

I have zero chain slap with XO transmission on my new SB 140 though.

Not sure what the difference was, clutch resistance felt the same pushing both cages by hand.
  • 20
FrankS29 (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:24)
 @Ty927: A lot of the chain slap from AXS comes from the B-Knuckle swinging free since there is no cable housing keeping it somewhat under control.

Transmission solved that issue as it's direct mount and eliminated the B-Knuckle, keeping the derailleur much more stable.
  • 30
rltw2-14 FL (Jun 5, 2025 at 6:20)
 @FrankS29: Transmission didn't solve anything, it's bike dependent. There is still significant chain slap on some bikes that run Transmission.
  • 10
FrankS29 (Jun 5, 2025 at 6:28)
 @rltw2-14: Read what I wrote again. Did I write that it eliminated all chain slap?

Or, did I write that it eliminated the B-Knuckle, thus eliminating the issue of the B-Knuckle flopping all over the place?
  • 101
RunToTheHills FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:16)
 Its a no from me
  • 135
jackalopebikes (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:37)
 Innovation unrivaled by anyone, except SRAM in 2018. But it’s worse.
  • 80
atrokz FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:40)
 "- Still has a derailleur hanger" - This is a good thing, Dario.
  • 40
badam (Jun 4, 2025 at 15:26)
 @mikekazimer @Dario-DiGiulio I'd be curious your guys' take on how this lack of clutch plays with bikes that have large amounts of chain growth in their suspension. It seems like a non-friction based system like this might free up the suspension a bit more. Also, I didn't notice any discussion of whether the new system resulted in any more dropped chains, or if it was purely a chain slap thing.
  • 30
combfilter (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:04)
 "My one real gripe here is the lack of a trigger shift function," --- This Sucks.. If you ever started to use the "trigger" shifting on previous shimano you fell in love with it. Was really hoping they would offer this.

Springs vs. Clutch is not that big of deal. Matter of fact the one thing that was not great about previous shimano clutches was that if you were cross chained up in the 50, the clutch would make it feel horrible and put a lot of tension on how pedaling would feel.. If you released the clutch, it would feel much better.
  • 20
RWRides (Jun 4, 2025 at 17:29)
 Yes it is a big deal. Springs are not dampers. Damping is what is needed. That requires the friction from a clutch. The clutch rd is one of the best inventions for mtb and this is not going to work out well for shimano. mmw. The solution is a better clutch.
  • 30
Stevel-Knievel (Jun 4, 2025 at 12:17)
 Chain slap is why I ditched GX AXS, the derailleur pretty much smashed itself to bits, crashing into the b-screw stop - swinging about under it's own weight with no damping effect of the looped cable outer, it bent multiple b-screws and required the 'hall lock' modification/bodge just to shut the thing up, by which time the bushes we're worn beyond repair and it went in the bin. SRAM addressed this issue when developing t-type, but it seems Shimano didn't get the memo and will have to go though this learning process too..

For me, it's now strictly good old reliable and quiet cable shifting for MTB, but would be happy to run Di2/AXS on the road/gravel.
  • 10
Kookonabike (Jun 4, 2025 at 13:00)
 Agree on the b knuckle chain slap thing! Got GX AXS by default on a new bike last year and hated the noise. Found this o-ring replacement video which worked surprisingly so well…

youtu.be/prh8vHPw7z8?si=3TzszDMqY5quLyVt
  • 10
Stevel-Knievel (Jun 4, 2025 at 13:22)
 @Kookonabike: Yeah. I ran the o-ring trick for a while, but even that got chewed up after a while. I ended up using a thin copper washer which lasted until the bushes gave out and it started skipping gears. I'll try to find and share the washer dimensions..
  • 20
Stevel-Knievel (Jun 4, 2025 at 13:32)
 10 x 16 x 1mm copper sealing washer apparently:

www.mtbr.com/threads/3-13-solution-for-sram-eagle-axs-derailleur-noise.1214185
  • 30
dphreaner (Jun 4, 2025 at 12:36)
 great review. Here is santa barbara where our trails are made up of 1 to 2 foot rocks with almost no dirt, the Transmission pulley assembly lasts a month or two without bending. I have replaced mine 4 times in 6 months due to rock strikes on Tunnel Trail. I think the body is so strong the only weak part is the pully assembly and it really hangs low in most gears. the only derailluer that has lasted a year for me is the shadow shimano style. Older SRAM ones hung so far lateral to the frame that they were disposable too. The new XRT shape sounds like it might be made for our rocky trails.
  • 52
TinuKu (Jun 4, 2025 at 14:34)
 XTR - the one I have been waiting for! Fast, precise, tactile feel and great ergonomics! This is going to replace my Transmission drivetrain tomorrow!
I have been riding Transmission on my XC/Downcountry bike for about 8 months, and I'm sorry to say that I'm hating it. It shifts super slow (it is reeaalllllyyyy that slow), there's always this one gear that will act out, no matter how I fine tune it, and the shift paddle is, simply said, bad. It took me forever to adjust it in a way that is easily usable, and I'm still not comfortable with it. I never liked the looks of the Transmission derailleur either, but that I got used to.
Riding tight up-and-down roller coaster trails here in the Emmental in Switzerland is really not what Transmission was made for. There's one comment in the video that struck me as incredibly true though - Transmission is made for E-Bikes! Absolutely! But in this case, go compare it with Shimano's Linkglide system, which is made for E-Bikes too, and still shifts tons faster with smoother gear transition under full load.
Great job with the new XTR drivetrain Shimano. You have me with this one!
Disclaimer: I'm just a small bike shop owner and I generally think that both brands are making awesome products. But I had to get that one off my chest. Transmission is for many people, but definitely not for me.
  • 30
notthatslow (Jun 4, 2025 at 18:20)
 It’s funny, one of my favourite things about old XTR Di2 is that it WASN’T wireless.
What are we getting compared to mechanical here? More weight, more battery charging, wireless UNreliability, for similar shifting and way more money. All to ditch one cable. Make it make sense.
  • 10
Hugou4BdaD (Jun 4, 2025 at 22:03)
 I think that they're going to release a mechanical version. There is a patent about it if I'm not mistaken.
  • 10
notthatslow (Jun 6, 2025 at 23:22)
 @Hugou4BdaD:
Oh good, now we can all get that 9t sprocket everyone has been pining for
  • 30
JudgeJor (Jun 4, 2025 at 22:58)
 500$ cassette and 666$ derailleur f$ck me! That's more than 1k for parts I typically need to replace yearly.

I wish Shimano would make a proper enduro cassette. Gears 1-2-3(?) steel and the rest aluminum, so exactly the opposite of the the XT. Why? On an enduro you spend most of the pedaling on the small gears which wear down the fastest. 100% aluminum would do as well.
  • 50
Newgame2113 (Jun 5, 2025 at 3:10)
 Cons - Pronounced chain slap - Still has a derailleur hanger Sir,these are actually pros to me. I hate the UGH and i hate the stupid clutches. Thank you shimano!
  • 30
basic-ti-hardtail (Jun 5, 2025 at 6:39)
 Preference: I’d stick with XT mechanical or SRAM 90 mechanical and put the extra money towards wheels, brakes, suspension…

But if I had to choose one of these systems right now I’d probably go with XTR 9-45 GS. I’m currently running XT/XTR 11 speed with an e13 9-46 Helix cassette on my lighter bike, so I already know this range works for me. Compared to 12 speed XT on my bigger bike, the reduced chain slap is noticeable. Both of those drivetrains have been so easy to live with and nearly flawless… why would I shell out so much more money to ‘upgrade’?

More thoughts:

I have a feeling we will see the new XT sooner vs. later. I’ll always pick XT over XTR as performance tends to be the same with only a mild weight difference and a rather dramatic price difference. With sram, I go for XO1… which costs a bit more but I find the overall quality and durabilty vs. GX tends to be worth it.

If I were buying a new bike that came with Xo1 transmission, I’d probably keep it and really like it… but I would consider a switch to SRAM 90.
If I bought a bike with this new XTR, I’d sell the shifter and derailleur and use XT mechanical instead. The same cross compatibility that makes it easy to upgrade also makes it easy to downgrade!
  • 20
trhoppe (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:49)
 Oh yea, I'm here for the next version of this which is XT. I've forever been an XTR shifter, XT derailleur, and XT cassette sort of person, as the shifter ergonomics are better on the mechanical shifter. For XT Di2, the shifter is going to be the same, and I'll gladly take the slightly heavier derailleur and cassette for the durability. And my mechanical XT will hum along just fine for the rest of this season until the XT Di2 drops for next. Sweeeeeet.
  • 86
Mcoppen FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:46)
 Really underwhelmed by this. Shimano have basically just released AXS, 7 years to late and after Sram have already released a better version in Transmission. One of the key selling points of Transmission for me is the fact I have never had to touch it in 2k miles of riding, zero maintenance required. Shimano having a b tension and the need to adjust it regularly as mentioned in the review is terrible. You might as well save a fortune and just go mechanical.
  • 53
xlcheese (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:06)
 When shifting an electric system we are only clicking buttons. With the right positioning of the Transmission pod under the bar and further forward my thumb naturally rests between the two buttons. I barely have to move my thumb to shift up or down. This is beneficial for longer mtb rides where you're tired and hands are a little weak. This shifter by shimano does not makes sense to me. Why mimick a mechanical shifter?
  • 20
skimons (Jun 4, 2025 at 13:06)
 @mikekazimer thanks for the review! out of curiosity some questions: can it pair / sync with your head unit (like garmin) with similar functions as the road version / sram axs? and does it have a derailleur cage lock for easy removal of the rear wheel? do they still have the same chain lock as before?
  • 30
iduckett FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 13:19)
 'Granny shiftin' not double clutchin' like you should'

Removing the clutch is a backwards move IMO. How often are we smacking clutch bodies off derailleurs?? Chain retention is #1 in my book...
  • 20
heinous (Jun 4, 2025 at 14:46)
 Leaving proper tuning and trimming to the user is a massive advantage over Transmission, where it’s so totally reliant on both frame/hub tolerances and sram’s own QC tolerances (which have been famously *variable* forever) it’s almost normal to hear people having to hack spacers etc together to make it work.
  • 50
aarong133 (Jun 4, 2025 at 17:09)
 @mikekazimer @Dario-DiGiulio Great review and video. Thank you for the awesome content.
  • 30
joni0001984 (Jun 4, 2025 at 22:04)
 Just bought a M8130 linkglide drivetrain for few hundred and can’t be happier. Weighs as much as GX transmission, but the extra weight comes from the extremely solid cassette, instead of batteries with corrosive pins.
  • 20
TonyFlynn (Jun 5, 2025 at 0:41)
 For me… any wireless shifting that in all other aspects is the same as mechanical… to me at least, offers no benefit…

Same shift, same adjustment screws, going to break in the same ways, heavier (I presume), more expensive…

I only see re-packaging rather than real development.
  • 20
blondemoustache (Jun 5, 2025 at 7:34)
 What happened to the old standards of testing at Pinkbike? No decibel meter for chainslap noise? One tiny drop, and no riding commentary? A 10 second climb and the bike was unmic'ed so we couldn't even hear it working? And if you already have your mind made up before the testing, where's the fun in that! It was clearly a recap and not a test. I was so ready to watch a proper head-to-head between the two drivetrain giants. Y'all need to go rewatch some Levy videos.
  • 20
Hugou4BdaD (Jun 5, 2025 at 9:45)
 In the video was very clear that the Sram RD was much more noisy, with a clear chain slap. I don't know how they concluded that this XTR was louder and the "clutch" did not work as expected.
  • 106
danielomeara FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:40)
 Shimano playing ketchup, puts out a product to compete with the original axs lineup.
  • 20
schweatyschwinn FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:17)
 Excited to see future revisions with a lower price and better chain slap reduction. I moved back to XTR after Shimano updated their cable retention. I've found XT and XTR to be quieter than the X01.
  • 30
NuckaMan (Jun 4, 2025 at 13:32)
 Well balanced and tensioned cable actuated XTR Shifter/Derail provides the pinnacle in MTB drivetrain shifting performance. Bonus, no batteries required!
  • 10
redfoxrun (Jun 4, 2025 at 20:33)
 Yes
  • 20
Pitiuso (Jun 4, 2025 at 13:36)
 I’m just happy Shimano is finally coming out with 160mm cranks. I know Hope and other brands have shorter cranks but it’s hard to beat Shimano on value. Hopefully they release new SLX cranks in 155mm
  • 30
dungeonbeast (Jun 4, 2025 at 18:28)
 Rumors that SLX might be gone completely soon.
  • 20
Hugou4BdaD (Jun 4, 2025 at 22:05)
 @dungeonbeast: some says that it's already gone. If you check their website, they removed the list with the SLX components.
  • 10
Pitiuso (Jun 5, 2025 at 2:54)
 @dungeonbeast: Interesting! I wonder if they are going to release a new groupset to compete with SRAM GX.
  • 21
GZMS (Jun 5, 2025 at 6:09)
 @Pitiuso: its called xt
  • 20
Hugou4BdaD (Jun 5, 2025 at 6:51)
 @GZMS: XT is at X0 level. GX is somewhere between SLX and Deore.
  • 10
oneheckler (Jun 4, 2025 at 15:30)
 Been using shimano for all my life ,mostly the xtr ,the adjustable 9 speed trigger was a miss in the latest ones ,that was a so simple thing and yet what a thing you could adjust the lateral reach ,and it really did make a huge difference,and the shifting on that group was like holding a now new car paddle shifting gears ,when going down a cassete ,I still use it and it still makes me thing f this this is like butter and fast ,they didn’t have a clutch at the derailleur ,and that was to bad ,different times ,but the shift in gears it was and I say it still is unmatched ,and people say that XT is the same as XTR ,they are stupid ,the feelings on an XTR group compared to an XT are a different thing ,of course you could live with it but a whole different experience,the touching everything,I had both ,in terms of electronics the SRAM is slow it is ,like you don’t lag in pedaling but the system do that for you ,it’s strange ,and clutch problems ,it is a so easy an cheaper thing to do ,just buy one and change it ,the derailleurs will fell just like new,and if you take an XTR and an XT one rear derailleurs apart ,there are very different (small ok)things in terms of construction and material,long live shimano ,but that prices ,give 11 speed and cable
  • 10
redfoxrun (Jun 4, 2025 at 20:29)
 The adjustable clutch with on off switch is what differentiated mechanical 12 speed shimano and a big reason I never was tempted to try anything else.

Even If they developed an electronic 12 speed with a clutch, I’d just stick to mechanical, doesn’t bother me one bit it came out in 2019.
  • 10
Gibbersticks (Jun 5, 2025 at 0:19)
 I would prefer Shimano's semi wireless like their road Di2 if the derailleur was less porky. Why does the battery have to be on the derailleur? With most bikes that would have this level of drivetrain will have internal frame storage. Just make the battery mount there where it's accessible and slim down the derailleur.
  • 10
thekaiser (Jun 5, 2025 at 16:52)
 The big benefit of semi-wireless on the road is that they also need to power the front derailleur. For a 1x system like this, it seems like less of an upside, and you introduce the downside of having to route a somewhat fragile cable around a suspension linkage.
  • 10
dsciulli19 (Jun 5, 2025 at 4:22)
 Ok now that we have wireless groupsets in both of the big brands can we focus on a gearbox option that will actually work for the masses? The shifting mechanism needs to be somewhere other than dangling below the chainstay just waiting to get smacked with something.
  • 10
damientheo (Jun 7, 2025 at 9:26)
 number of times I've ripped off a derailluer from just a hit = 0.
number of times I've gotten a stick jammed somewhere in there that rips the cages around and does or does not take the rest of the derialleur with it = 10+.
The wedge shape thing is cute but not necessary just like ScAMS stupid unbreakable UDH.
May be irrelevant but had to say it somewhere
  • 10
S1am (Jun 10, 2025 at 8:13)
 Wireless shifting is dumb. My SRAM transmission routinely tells me I have a full charge only to be completely depleted 10 miles later. If I didn't carry a spare battery on my (less than 1 year old) bike I'd have done several rides this season on an accidental single speed. Also, having to have a smartphone app that connects to the internet in order to ride a bike on dirt trails in the woods is dumb.
  • 10
schoefli (Dec 28, 2025 at 4:07)
 @Dario-DiGiulio: You mention Linkglide and Transmission performing better under load. How about chain slap, how would you rate Sram Transmission (mechanical and electronic), the older Sram Eagle drivetrains, Shimano HG+, and Linkglide from best to worst? The new Shimano electronic drivetrains seem to be worst from this perspective.

I haven‘t found any comparisons focusing on chain slap alone, would be great to read about your experiences!

chrismac70 FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:15)
 Let’s see the XT and depression versions and see what the price difference are v sram

bmied31 (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:32)
 Seems like a 9-46 cassette with slightly smaller chainring would be the best of both worlds. I loved that range on my 11 speed stuff with the shitty e13 cassette. For me I'll stick with my Transmission.

StanWu01 (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:42)

Hsawaknow46 (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:42)

dividebyzero (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:44)
 No clutch is a weird move but it seems like they are squarely targeting the XC camp. For what I ride Transmission is the obvious choice, strength, quality shifts to a harder gear over speed, and chain retention.

thekaiser (Jun 5, 2025 at 16:54)

smootherthenyou (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:49)

EMan1382 (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:10)

wibblywobbly (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:20)

Hope Shimano has a new ebike motor coming becuase that’s their last hope.

We need competition. We need someone to keep sram honest and come out with something that shifts fast and doesn’t cost 2000$
  • 50
Zachmozach (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:48)
 To be fair, their cable actuated stuff shifts fast and doesn't cost that much.

no-good-ideas (Jun 4, 2025 at 13:23)
 Current gen XT/XTR does exactly this.

People just want something new for the sake of it.
  • 44
st-lupo (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:23)
 Not sure I get the hate, I think Shimano knocked it outta the park with this!
It's _wireless_: check
It's got batteries: check
It's more expensive: check
Oh, it shifts: check

It does everything an MTB gear system should do: shift and signal to everyone that you have (or had) fat stacks of cash. If there is a small nit-pick, Shimano missed out on an opportunity to include an OLED screen on the shifter to display your current gear, total number of shifts, msrp, and a radio synchronized clock or something.
  • 44
davidvonslingshot (Jun 4, 2025 at 13:03)
 Yeeeeeears late to the party. Blaming everything from shipping issues, covid, warehouse fires..... wompwomp. Good that its actually wireless But SRAM has been handing Shimano's arse back to them for years. I am curious to try the brakes.... but hey, you can't get em. Tisk
  • 20
HB208 (Jun 4, 2025 at 13:19)
 SRAM's MO is releasing products rapidly and then patenting everything they can... and don't forget every generation of product is not compatible with old generations.
  • 11
Wthomas (Jun 4, 2025 at 18:20)
 I don’t know if it makes sense to go wireless with shimano. Their cable stuff is bulletproof. I’m still on 11 spd. Never had a single ghost shift and I’m bad about replacing my cable. I was on the same cable for 3 years and only replaced it because of time.
  • 11
SchalkMarais (Jun 4, 2025 at 21:30)
 I’ve been toying with the idea to move back to a Shimano drivetrain, but the direct mount SRAM has is conceptually still the best.
Who needs wireless shifting !!
They could at least have thrown in a direct mount solution…
  • 11
imalers (Jun 4, 2025 at 22:50)
 I think Shimano nailed this.

Virtual every "Adventure" oriented MTB rider that I know has completely sworn of electronic shifting forever in any format. This isn't for them.

Electronic shifting has advantages for "Workout/Race" riders.
  • 10
EnduroManiac (Jun 5, 2025 at 5:25)

  • 85
bermtownusa (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:02)
 faster!!! And backwards compatible
  • 75
novajustin (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:07)
 long article and maybe it's covered there, but why not mount directly to the UDH? or did sram patent that mounting method?
  • 112
neimbc (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:09)
 Sram did not patent, hoping maunfacturers would all jump on board, but no way is Shimano going to have anything compatible with Sram.
  • 135
novajustin (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:14)
 @neimbc: it’s a shame really. I was holding out for today to see what this announcement from Shimano was going to be about. I assumed it was going to be wireless but I wanted to see if they were going to have any other updates, udh mounting being one of them. I really think direct udh mounting is the future and which is why I’ll be shopping for Eagle 90 now and moving away from Shimano for the first time.
  • 1613
z-man (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:45)
 @novajustin: Because UDH is dumb. I prefer a sacrificial hanger to a sacrificial derailleur.
  • 101
JSTootell (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:09)
 @z-man: UDH IS a sacrificial hangar.

You are talking about the direct mount, which just uses the UDH holes.
  • 33
novajustin (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:17)
 @z-man: every part on the sram t-type derailleur is replaceable. they did this because they moved away from hangers. this really is the way as it takes out all of the guess work in hi/lo limit adjustments and just flat out works. i've never been on the sram bandwagon, but that alone has me leaning towards eagle 90.
  • 30
regularotbr (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:39)
 @novajustin: fwiw after riding eagle 90 and XO transmission, I find I actually prefer Eagle 90. It seems to shift better and its definitely a faster shifting system. Was incredibly easy to setup too...
  • 20
novajustin (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:22)
 @regularotbr: all of the reviews i have read on it have been positive. i'm not sold on wireless as i'm not the best at remembering to charge things so e90 would be the direction i go. the setup looks so easy and the lack of having to dial in the limits really appeals to me. i find i have to regularly check on my xt 12 speed limit screws as my shifting will get out of whack pretty quickly. with shimano not releasing anything ground breaking with this release has me thinking it's going to be a long time before they catch up with the simplicity of sram which really has me wanting to jump ship. i've been a solid shimano user for so many years, but i just can't ignore that e90 kit anymore.
  • 10
regularotbr (Jun 4, 2025 at 13:58)
 @novajustin: I switched from an XT groupset when my freehub exploded. The B limit always backed out after 3-4 chunky rides and as much as I liked the shifting, got really tired of it and wanted the same parts across all my and wife's bikes. I had been wanting to try Eagle 90 since I liked AXS, but didn't wanna spend whatever it was for it on this bike and I haven't been disappointed. As a sub-par rider and mechanic, the lack of a hanger, tension screws, and ease of setup has left me very happy! It'll go on all my other bikes going forward for sure!
  • 20
z-man (Jun 4, 2025 at 14:18)
 @JSTootell: Sorry, you’re correct about UDH. I still disagree about replacement parts though. Those should always be available regardless of mounting type. It’s still easier and cheaper to replace a hanger than rebuild a derailleur.
  • 10
novajustin (Jun 4, 2025 at 15:37)
 @z-man: i don't know about you, but in the past when i have ruined a hanger the derailleur is also usually pretty mangled as well. it takes a lot of force to break a hanger and usually the derailleur is going to bend or break as well. a replacement cage for an eagle 90 derailleur is 70 bucks and from what i have seen, taking apart the derailleur is simple.
  • 30
Treyster8 (Jun 4, 2025 at 15:42)
 The guys at Flow Mountain bike did a great YouTube explainer on this.

Shimano still value hangers and have ‘protected the mech’ giving it a low profile, side knock protection (Like SRAM) and also let it release in a rearward path to move it out of harms way.
  • 10
JSTootell (Jun 4, 2025 at 17:22)
 @novajustin: this is strictly n=1. But I rarely bend hangers, maybe one every couple of years. Never broke a derailleur. But during the period I was running GX AXS (NOT Transmission) I probably went through 4 hangers. As soon as I swapped back to X01 mechanical, no more bent hangers.

Coincidence? Maybe. But anecdata none the less.
  • 10
novajustin (Jun 4, 2025 at 21:32)
 @JSTootell: almost like the electric motor gives no fcks about your hanger whereas maybe your thumb feels sorry for putting that much force through?
  • 10
JSTootell (Jun 5, 2025 at 7:16)
 @novajustin: They all bent in a crash.

Either it is coincidence, or the motor clutch doesn't deactivate effectively in a hit.

Gregstinson (Dec 18, 2025 at 17:38)

needmoregears (Jun 4, 2025 at 9:54)

alpinepedaler (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:01)

bman33 (Jun 4, 2025 at 12:10)

  • 12
PivotMach (Jun 5, 2025 at 8:57)
 @bman33: Not really, the market has spoken and Shimano is barely in the game from SLX on up. This is not an emotional statement, just look at the OEM bike specs for any major manufacturer. I truly wish Shimano was on their A-game, a 50/50 market place would be healthy for us all. They appear distracted or complacent, something has been off there for the last decade or so it feels that way.
  • 10
bman33 (Jun 5, 2025 at 10:13)
 @PivotMach: you are looking at OEM spec from our high end, niche market. Globally at the commuter level and even very low end 'bike shop' type bikes,Shimano is still a juggernaut. This is true even in many Western /1st World countries at the commuter and department store level.

And yes, my comment reflects Aplinepedaler's 'preferred' which is a 100% subjective terminology. Many do in fact prefer SRAM. However, many also 'prefer' Shimano, regardless of OEM market share or not. An extreme version of this is on the road side with Campy. Does Campy have much OEM market? Of course not. But there still is a decent population of road riders who 'prefer' Campy.

becik (Jun 4, 2025 at 10:16)
 I'm pretty sure sram T-type chain last longer than Shimano's XTR.
Add to it random shiffting from shimano. (under power)

I will keep my Sram.

Especialy for E-bikes
  • 50
atrokz FL (Jun 4, 2025 at 11:05)
 T-Type chains are some of the longest lasting chains, with a concession given to efficiency.... they are also some of the least efficient chains on the market. The YBN SLA MK12 is said to be 40% more efficient, which is huge. This doesn't apply to the lower end flattop chains as they suffer from both wear and efficiency. Zerofriction has a bunch of information on it.
